events.title: Button Book Club
events.description: Do you love to read but hate deadlines? Or are you just too busy to attend traditional book club meetings? If so, our Button Book Club is perfect for you! Each month there will be a new book to read and a brand new pin to collect! How It Works Pick Your Book! Each month will feature a new Easy/Picture, Beginning Reader, Middle Grade, and Teen book. Simply pick which level you want to read and check out the book! Share Your Thoughts! Come back anytime during the month and write/draw your favorite part of the book. Get Your Pin! Each book will have it's own special, collectible pin. Participate every month to collect them all! Plus, get a free lanyard with your first pin to show them off! December Books: Easy/Picture: Pop! Goes the Nursery Rhyme by Betsy Bird Beginning Readers: First Flight by SJ King Middle Grade: The Library of Unruly Treasures by Jeanne Birdsall Teen: His Face is the Sun by Michelle Jabes Corpora

events.title: Teen Passive Book Club: Contemporary
events.description: This month's teen passive book club theme is contemporary! Our featured title is We Are Okay by Nina LaCour: After leaving California to go to college in New York, Marin must face the truth about what happened in the final weeks of summer when her friend comes to visit. Grab a copy (or any alternate title) in the teen area & an activity packet!

events.description: Button Book Club is a passive book club, where participants read a book inspired by the monthly theme, fill in a brief worksheet (2 questions) and collect button. With a curated selection of books on everything from Ru-Paul to Doll Pardon and Doris Day to Lost Cincinnati concert venues, there is something for everyone. Something else spark your interest? We can't wait to hear what it is. Please see staff if you have any questions.

events.title: StoryWalk® with Dougherty Station and San Ramon Libraries
events.description: Get some exercise and read the latest story installed at the StoryWalk in Central Park. The winter themed story is called Except Antarctica, written and illustrated by Todd Sturgell. Follow turtle and learn about animals found on every continent except Antarctica!The San Ramon StoryWalk® is a permanent feature in San Ramon’s Central Park. The 20-station project features a new book four times a year. The San Ramon StoryWalk® project is a partnership with the Rotary Club of San Ramon, the City of San Ramon, the San Ramon Library Foundation, and the Library. The Central Park StoryWalk® begins near the Alcosta parking lot to the right of the playground, ending at the Community Center’s rear fountain. The StoryWalk Project was created by Anne Ferguson of Montpelier, VT and developed in collaboration with the Kellogg-Hubbard Library. StoryWalk is a registered service mark owned by Ms. Ferguson.

events.title: Nonfiction Navigators Book Club - Feature Topic: LOVE
events.description: Come join our Nonfiction Navigators Book Club, meeting the second Wednesday every other month at 12:00 PM. Share your book discoveries and favorites on selected nonfiction topics. Bring your own lunch or snack and opinions. Choose a title from the 2026 Feature Topic Book suggestion list (COMING SOON) or another nonfiction title from the library catalog & register for the program! March's book discussion topic will be LOVE! Love is defined as an intense feeling of deep affection. It could also mean a great interest and pleasure in something. What do you think of when you think of Love? Compassion? Caring? Kindness? Choose a title from the LOVE Book Suggestion List or another from the library catalog and share your discoveries! Looking for upcoming Nonfiction Navigators Book Club Topics? Check out our upcoming topic selections here (COMING SOON) and place a hold on a book from the current book topic: LOVE. The theme for 2026 is Positive Emotions - Happiness, Love, Peaceful, Amused, Amazed, and Grateful. View, print or download a flyer of the 2026 book club dates and selections here. (COMING SOON)This program will be provided by Independence Regional Library staff. For more information email cbuchanan@cmlibrary.org or call 704-416-4800 opt. 4. Information about this Group The Nonfiction Navigators Book Club meets every other month to share favorite books on selected nonfiction topics. events.title: 🦥Polliwog Club🦡🦎🦦🦜🦉
events.description: Harris County Precinct One presents the Polliwog Club! The program will explore the animal kingdom and learn about mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, spiders, and insects. Each program will include a book/song…a small lesson with animal artifacts, and a craft. Join us every second Wednesday (except April) of each month. Join us for Polliwog Club on the second Wednesday of each month (except April) at 11 a.m.! A traveling naturalist from Precinct 1 will be here at the West University Branch Library to share a story and fascinating information with us about a particular habitat and the animals that live there. They will bring 3-4 animal artifacts from the featured habitat that the children will be able to see up close, and they will also have an opportunity to gently touch the artifacts if they would like to. Touching the objects is always optional. Hand sanitizer will be provided. This wildly fun, free Environmental Education Program is designed for preschoolers and up and their caregiver(s). Siblings are always welcome. No registration or tickets required.

events.title: Wednesday Afternoon Murder Mystery Book Club: Jar of Hearts
events.description: Are you a reader looking for a murder mystery to solve, a thrill to seek, or a story full of suspense? If you answer yes to any or all these questions, you must join Edgebrook Branch's Wednesday Afternoon Murder Mystery Book Club! For March, we are reading and discussing Jar of Hearts by Jennifer Hillier. "Angela was one of the most popular girls in her high school before she disappeared without a trace. Nobody ever knew what became of her—not Georgina, her best friend, nor Kaiser, who was close with both girls. Then, fourteen years later, Angela’s remains were discovered in the woods near Geo’s childhood home and Kaiser—who became a detective with the Seattle police department—finally learned the truth: Angela was a victim of Calvin James. To the authorities, Calvin was a notorious serial killer. But back in the day, Calvin was Geo’s first love. Their relationship bordered on obsession from the moment they met, right up until the night Angela was killed. Geo carried the secret of Calvin’s dark deeds until the evidence landed her in prison. But now, just as Geo is about to be released, new bodies start turning up—killed in the exact same manner as Angela...and soon Geo, Kaiser, and local law enforcement officials realize that what happened that fateful night is more complex and chilling than anyone could have imagined." - Description from MacMillan Publishers. Copies of the book will be available at Edgebrook Branch's reference desk starting on 3/12/26 while supplies last. AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Inquisitive Minds: Nonfiction Book Club
events.description: Join us on the 2nd Wednesday of each month at 3pm for Inquisitive Minds: Nonfiction Book Club! We will meet in the Community Room at SouthPark Regional Library. This month (March) we're discussing: The Wager: A Tale of Shipwreck, Mutiny and Murder by David Grann "From the #1 New York Times bestselling author of Killers of the Flower Moon and The Lost City of Z, a mesmerizing story of shipwreck, survival, and savagery, culminating in a court martial that reveals a shocking truth On January 28, 1742, a ramshackle vessel of patched-together wood and cloth washed up on the coast of Brazil. Inside were thirty emaciated men, barely alive, and they had an extraordinary tale to tell. They were survivors of His Majesty's Ship the Wager, a British vessel that had left England in 1740 on a secret mission during an imperial war with Spain. While the Wager had been chasing a Spanish treasure-filled galleon known as "the prize of all the oceans," it had wrecked on a desolate island off the coast of Patagonia. The men, after being marooned for months and facing starvation, built the flimsy craft and sailed for more than a hundred days, traversing 2500 miles of storm-wracked seas. They were greeted as heroes. But then ... six months later, another, even more decrepit craft landed on the coast of Chile. This boat contained just three castaways, and they had a very different story to tell. The thirty sailors who landed in Brazil were not heroes - they were mutineers. The first group responded with countercharges of their own, of a tyrannical and murderous captain and his henchmen. It became clear that while stranded on the island the crew had fallen into anarchy, with warring factions fighting for dominion over the barren wilderness. As accusations of treachery and murder flew, the Admiralty convened a court martial to determine who was telling the truth. The stakes were life-and-death-for whomever the court found guilty could hang. The Wager is a grand tale of human behavior at the extremes told by one of our greatest nonfiction writers. Grann's recreation of the hidden world on a British warship rivals the work of Patrick O'Brian, his portrayal of the castaways' desperate straits stands up to the classics of survival writing such as The Endurance, and his account of the court martial has the savvy of a Scott Turow thriller. As always with Grann's work, the incredible twists of the narrative hold the reader spellbound. Most powerfully, he unearths the deeper meaning of the events, showing that it was not only the Wager's captain and crew who were on trial - it was the very idea of empire." This program will be provided by the SouthPark Regional Library staff. Click here to find the book: https://cmlibrary.bibliocommon... Upcoming Reads:April: Cue the Sun – The Invention of Reality Show TV by Emily NussbaumMay: Abundance by Ezra Klein & Derek Thompson

events.title: Book Club for Tweens!
events.description: East Point Library is excited to launch its first tween book club! Join us as we launch our very first Tween Book Club! Starting now, tweens ages 8-13 are invited to check out our first book selection: "Pippa Park Raises Her Game" by Erin Yun. At Lakeview, Pippa juggles old and new friends, an unrequited crush, and the pressure to perform academically and athletically while keeping her past and her family's laundromat a secret from her elite new classmates—a fun, feel-good story about friendship, basketball, and finding your confidence. The East Point Library has copies available in paperback, or you can check out this book as a downloadable audiobook and an eBook. Whether you love to read, want to make new friends, or are looking for a fun after-school activity, our Tween Book Club is a great way to connect with other book lovers and discover new stories together. Mark your calendars—our very first book club get-together is happening on March 11h at 4:00 pm! I can’t wait to chat about this book with you!

events.title: Freedom to Read: Preteen Read Aloud Book Club (Esperanza Rising)
events.description: Big stories. Bold characters. Zero pressure. Join us twice a month for a high-energy read-aloud experience designed just for preteens! We’ll dive into exciting books together—laughing, predicting plot twists, debating characters’ choices, and bringing stories to life through discussion and interactive activities.This club removes common barriers to reading by making stories accessible to everyone. Whether your preteen is a strong reader, a reluctant reader, or prefers listening over reading independently, this space centers the joy of storytelling. Participants will also have access to digital audiobooks, making it easy to continue building literacy skills anytime, anywhere. March's Book Selection: Esperanza Rising, by Pam Muñoz Ryan "Esperanza thought she'd always live with her family on their ranch in Mexico - she'd always have fancy dresses, a beautiful home, and servants. But a sudden tragedy forces Esperanza and Mama to flee to California during the Great Depression, and to settle in a camp for Mexican farm workers. Esperanza isn't ready for the hard labor, financial struggles, or lack of acceptance she now faces. When their new life is threatened, Esperanza must find a way to rise above her difficult circumstances - Mama's life, and her own, depend on it." Meeting Dates: Wed. 3/11 & 3/25

events.title: Pages & Purpose Book Club
events.description: Pages & Purpose is a book club focusing on thoughtful nonfiction books by black authors. Join us on the 2nd and 4th Wednesday of each month from 6-7pm. On March 11th and 25th we'll be discussing Notes of a Native Son by James Baldwin. His breakthrough essay collection made him the voice of his generation. Ranging over Harlem in the 1940s, movies, novels, his preacher father and his experiences of Paris, they capture the complexity of black life at the dawn of the civil rights movement with effervescent wit and prophetic wisdom.We meet in the Conference Room. Registration is required. Happy Reading!

events.title: Virtual Next Chapter Book Club: Top Story
events.description: You're invited to join the Saint Paul Public Library's Next Chapter Book Club as we read Top Story by Kelly Yang. Since the fall of 2016, Saint Paul Public Library has been a Next Chapter Book Club affiliate. Next Chapter Book Club offers community-based book clubs for adolescents and adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities. Our members (including some who cannot read) love the way books transport them to different worlds where they meet interesting characters and learn about exciting new things. Taking that journey with a group of friends makes it even more delightful and fun. Join us online (click this link to connect to the virtual book club) Wednesdays 5-6 pm until we reach the end of the book. Estimated end date in March. Contact Erin at erin.zolotukhin-ridgway@ci.stpaul.mn.us with questions. This book will be shared online during book club.

events.title: Fantasy and Science Fiction Book Club: Iron Gold by Pierce Brown
events.description: Join us on the second Wednesday each month to discuss a fantasy or science fiction book! --- This month's title is Iron Gold by Pierce Brown.A decade ago, Darrow was the hero of the revolution he believed would break the chains of the Society, and abolish the color-coded caste system. But the Rising has shattered everything, and brought endless war. New foes emerge to threaten what has been earned, and throughout the worlds other destinies entwine with Darrow's to change his fate forever. A young Red girl flees tragedy in her refugee camp, and achieves for herself a new life she could never have imagined. An ex-soldier broken by grief is forced to steal the most valuable thing in the galaxy -- or pay with his life. And Lysander au Lune, the heir in exile to the Sovereign, wanders the stars with his mentor, Cassius, haunted by the loss of the world that Darrow transformed, and dreaming of what will rise from its ashes.

events.title: Spectacular Sixth Grade Book Club
events.description: Spectacular Sixth Grade Book Club is a book discussion group for 6th graders. If you love reading books and discussing them with a group, this is the place for you! Some months we'll also have an activity to go along with the discussion. We'll meet on the second Wednesday of the month. This month, we are reading Alone by Megan E. Freeman. For our first meeting, we'll hand out the first month's book and introduce ourselves and the book club. Please bring your Alameda County Library Card with you to the meeting. An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) business days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Music For Self-Care: Share Your Favorite Song!
events.description: A very popular program here at Rogers Park that is all about music appreciation combined with music therapy. As an alternative to a book club, this is a song club, where each patron chooses one song to share with others in a communal setting, then we each go around the room discussing our reactions, thoughts and overall feelings as we listened. Share a memory you associate specifically with the song, tell us why it has a special place in your life. Bring one favorite track to play for others. We'll have access to YouTube so all you have to do is bring a title. We will take turns playing a piece of music to the group, then telling why it matters to us. This is a monthly event on the 2nd Wednesday of every month, so we encourage regular attendees to return with other songs!AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event. Image provided by staff

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: Whiskey Tender
events.description: Join the Oriole Park branch for a discussion of Whiskey Tender, by Deborah Jackson Taffa. "Whiskey Tender is a memoir of family and survival, coming-of-age on and off the reservation, and the frictions between being raised to strive towards the American dream while also coming into an understanding of how the narratives of the Quechan Nation and Laguna Pueblo heritages have been excluded from the central mythologies and structures of America"-- A limited number of copies are available at the branch. events.title: The In-Person Book Club - Evening Edition
events.description: Join us on the 2nd Wednesday of each month at 7:00 pm for The In-Person Davidson Branch Book Club! This month we will be discussing House in the Cerulean Sea by TJ Klune. This is the library's Community Read selection for March. I will have free copies available at our January and February meetings. From Publisher's Weekly: "Quirk and charm give way to a serious exploration of the dangers of complacency in this delightful, thought-provoking Orwellian fantasy from Klune. Caseworker Linus Baker of the Department in Charge of Magical Youths (DICOMY) believes he is doing right by the preternaturally gifted children placed in DICOMY-sanctioned orphanages. But Linus begins to question DICOMY's methods when the ominous Extremely Upper Management tasks Linus with evaluating the isolated Marsyas Island Orphanage and reporting not only on the island's extraordinary children--among them a female gnome, a blob of uncertain species who wants to be a bellhop, and a shy teenage boy who turns into a small dog when startled--but also on the orphanage master, Arthur Parnassus. The bonds Linus forms with the children and the romantic connection he feels for Arthur set Linus on a path toward redemption for the unwitting harm he caused as a cog in an uncaring bureaucratic machine. By turns zany and heartfelt, this tale of found family is hopeful to its core. Readers will revel in Klune's wit and ingenuity." This program will be provided by Davidson library staff. Information about this Group This book club reads across all genres. For a list of all 2026 selections, please click here: In-Person Book Club (Davidson) Looking for more technology resources? events.title: Dayton's Bluff Book Club
events.description: Dayton's Bluff Book Club is a great fit for folks who love reading diverse books by local authors and discussing them with a group of dedicated readers. We meet once a month on a Wednesday in the months of October, November, December, February, March, and April at 6 p.m. We have a limited supply of free books for book club participants that will be available for pick up at the Dayton's Bluff Branch Library when selections become finalized. Participants will be notified by email. Books will also be available to borrow at the library. The Dayton’s Bluff Book Club is a collaboration of the Metropolitan State University Library and the Dayton’s Bluff Branch of Saint Paul Public Library whose members live, work or attend classes on the East Side. We prioritize our selection of titles to those written by Minnesota authors, that have a local setting, or that are written by people of color and other diverse authors. Book discussions usually include issues related to social justice, equity, and anti-racism. We encourage book club members to use both the university and public libraries in pursuit of lifelong learning and the joy of reading. Discussions are frequently facilitated by authors, Metropolitan State faculty, or other guests from the community. Where They Last Saw HerWelcome - Dayton's Bluff Book Club - LibGuides at Metropolitan State University

events.title: Jewish American Fiction Book Club: The Last Dekrepitzer
events.description: Everyone is welcome to join our online (Zoom) book discussion group on Wednesday March 11, 2026. For more information and a Zoom invite, contact Davo Knepler at nuevodavos@yahoo.com.We will be discussing The Last Dekrepitzer by Howard Langer. The author will be joining us for the discussion. WINNER NATIONAL JEWISH BOOK AWARD The fiddler busking in the Columbus Circle subway station in 1965 is the Dekrepitzer Rebbe, the sole survivor of the obscure Dekrepitzer Hasidic sect known before the war for its rebbes' fiddling. The Last Dekrepitzer follows the life and spiritual quest of Shmuel Meir Lichtbencher a/k/a Sam Lightup, from his isolated shtetl in the mountains of southern Poland, where he is brought up to be the future rebbe, to the wharves in Naples, where he jams with Black soldiers waiting to ship home at the end of the war. Dressing him in the uniform and dog tags of an AWOL soldier, they smuggle him home to rural Mississippi. He lives for years among the Blacks, speaks Black English, preaches and plays the blues with the Brown Sugar Ramblers trio. His marriage to a Black woman, Lula Curtin, legal by Jewish law though forbidden under Mississippi law, results in a cross burning that forces them to flee to Manhattan. He plays on the streets of Harlem and Midtown with the Reverend Gary Davis, the great blind guitarist whose mission is saving souls for the next world. Shmuel Meir's devout wife, though she knows herself to be the Dekrepitzer Rebbitzen, is spurned by the Jewish community. Through it all, Shmuel Meir fiddles his prayers in defiance of God. But God gives the Dekrepitzer Rebbe no peace.For more information and a Zoom invite, contact Davo Knepler at nuevodavos@yahoo.com. Everyone is welcome to attend. Sponsored by Gan HaLev-the Jewish Congregation of San Geronimo Valley and the Fairfax Library, and assisted by the Jewish Library of San Francisco ADA Accommodation Request | Solicitud de Adaptación ADA

The 20-station project features a new book four times a year. The San Ramon StoryWalk® project is a partnership with the Rotary Club of San Ramon, the City of San Ramon, the San Ramon Library Foundation, and the Library. The Central Park StoryWalk® begins near the Alcosta parking lot to the right of the playground, ending at the Community Center’s rear fountain. The StoryWalk Project was created by Anne Ferguson of Montpelier, VT and developed in collaboration with the Kellogg-Hubbard Library. StoryWalk is a registered service mark owned by Ms. Ferguson.

About Community Read Community Read is Charlotte Mecklenburg Library’s annual, community-wide book club—an invitation to connect through a shared story. Each year’s book selection inspires programs, community conversations, and events that explore timely and meaningful themes. This year’s title The House in the Cerulean Sea by TJ Klune explores themes of found family, belonging, and self-acceptance. events.title: Getting Cozy Book Club
events.description: If you've never read a cozy mystery before or if you have read thousands, join us each month as we read and discuss the first book in a series. We will meet on the 2nd Thursday of the month at 10 am. What is a cozy mystery? The crime-solver is an amateur sleuth, who eavesdrops, gathers clues, and uses their native intelligence and knowledge of their community to solve the crime. Cozy mysteries are generally considered “gentle” books and most have no graphic violence. The setting is often a small town full of colorful characters.Series frequently have a prominent thematic element introduced by the detective's job, pet, or hobby.These books are plot and character driven with an emphasis on solving puzzles rather than suspense. Our chosen book with serve as our theme for the meeting, we will talk about other book series with a similar theme as well as the recipes, crafts, and more included in many cozies.

Join other adults learning English as a Second Language in this fun and engaging club. You'll read one book a month and then come discuss it with the rest of the club. Be ready to read your favorite passages to the group! If the books on this list look too easy, try our Advanced ESL Book Club. Here's the reading list: January: Ghost Boys February: The House on Mango Street March: Come on in April: Soul Lanterns May: The Breadwinner June: Paper Wishes July: The Lost Library August: The Birchbark House September: Rosa Parks, My Story October: The Elephant Girl November: A Night Divided December: The One and Only Ivan Any person needing a reasonable accommodation for a disability to access the benefits of the Library’s services, programs, or activities under the Americans with Disabilities Act should contact the Library by telephone at (972) 292-5669, by email at library@friscotexas.gov, or in writing, not less than five (5) business days prior to the program.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Chains (The Seeds of America Trilogy)
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read Chains (The Seeds of America Trilogy) by Laurie Halse Anderson. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Roberta Linsky. Meeting dates: February 26; March 5, 12, 19; April 2, 16; May 7. (No class March 26; April 9, 23, 30.)Meeting time: Thursdays from 10:00am - 11:00amWhere: Tables by the wall posters Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Book description:As the Revolutionary War begins, thirteen-year-old Isabel wages her own fight...for freedom. Promised freedom upon the death of their owner, she and her sister, Ruth, in a cruel twist of fate become the property of a malicious New York City couple, the Locktons, who have no sympathy for the American Revolution and even less for Ruth and Isabel. When Isabel meets Curzon, a slave with ties to the Patriots, he encourages her to spy on her owners, who know details of British plans for invasion. She is reluctant at first, but when the unthinkable happens to Ruth, Isabel realizes her loyalty belongs to whoever can provide her with freedom.

events.title: Civic Center Morning Mystery Book Group: 'Death of a Liar' by M.C. Beaton
events.description: The Civic Center Library's Morning Mystery Book Group will meet on Thursday, February 12th from 11:00am-12:00pm to discuss Death of a Liar by M.C. Beaton. This mystery book group generally meets on the 2nd Thursday of every month from 11:00-12:00n. All are welcome to join! Request a copy of the book online or ask at the Info Desk of the Civic Center Library (or call 415-473-6057) for assistance in placing a hold. Death of A Liar (book) or Death of A Liar (large print book) by M.C. Beaton Book #30 in the series set in a fictional town in the Scottish Highlands featuring police constable Hamish Macbeh. Sergeant Hamish Macbeth is alarmed to receive a report from a woman in the small village of Cronish in the Scottish Highlands. She has been brutally attacked and the criminal is on the loose. But upon further investigation, Hamish discovers that she was lying about the crime. So when the same woman calls him back about an intruder, he simply marvels at her compulsion to lie. This time, though, she is telling the truth. Her body is found in her home and Hamish must sort through all of her lies to solve the crime. - adapted from the publisher ADA Accommodation Request | Solicitud de Adaptación ADA

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL - On Book Banning
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Join us to share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. "On Book Banning" by Ira Wells SYNOPSIS: From the destruction of libraries in ancient Rome to today’s state-sponsored efforts to suppress LGBTQ+ literature, book bans arise from the impulse toward social control. In a survey of legal cases, literary controversies, and philosophical arguments, Ira Wells illustrates the historical opposition to the freedom to read and argues that today’s conservatives and progressives alike are warping our children’s relationship with literature and teaching them that the solution to opposing viewpoints is outright expurgation. At a moment in which our democratic institutions are buckling under the stress of polarization, On Book Banning is both rallying cry and guide to resistance for those who will always insist upon reading for themselves.

events.title: March Moraga Book Club
events.description: Join other book lovers for a lively discussion regarding Ruth Reichl's The Paris Novel:Stella, estranged from her mother Celia, inherits money for a trip to Paris. Reluctantly, she goes and lives modestly while visiting attractions. An impulse purchase of a vintage Dior dress leads her to meet Jules, an art expert who expands her horizons. This multi-layered narrative appeals to fans of art, culture, Paris and happy endings. Ruth Reichl is the New York Times bestselling author of five memoirs. She was editor in chief of Gourmet magazine, and previously served as restaurant critic for The New York Times, as well as food editor and restaurant critic for the Los Angeles Times.Note that Moraga Book Club is for adults ages 18 and up. It is also an in-person event at Moraga Library and online (through Zoom). Registration is required for online participation only.

events.title: Between the Bookends Book Club
events.library: Public Library of Youngstown and Mahoning County
events.description: We've all experienced a bit of burnout. Well, Sasha has had it. She's done with inane emails that aren't urgent and being part of the joyfulness employee program. She hasn't had a fun day, a day for herself in who knows how long. Follow Sasha in The Burnout by Sophia Kinsella as she works her way through it.

events.title: Read the World: A Middle Grade Book Club
events.description: Read the World is a book discussion group for youth in 6th to 9th grade. Each month we'll read a book set in a different country then enjoy conversations about the book as well as other cultures and experiences. We'll read from multiple categories, including fiction, nonfiction, graphic novels, novels in verse, and more. This month we'll be reading and discussing Temple Alley Summer by Sachiko Kashiwaba and set in Japan. Please register then stop by the library for a free copy of the book no earlier than one month before the discussion.One rainy night, Kazu sees a strange figure in a white kimono sneak out of his house--was he dreaming? Did he see a ghost? The next day at school, the very same person is sitting in his class--and all his friends are convinced that the ghost-girl Akari has been their friend for years. If that isn't weird enough, Kazu learns that his house is in the exact location of an ancient temple called Kimyō, which, legend has it, could bring the dead back to life! Kazu sets out to discover what happened to Kimyō Temple and if the rumors of its power are true. His investigation draws the unwanted attention of his neighbor Ms. Minakami and his mysterious new classmate Akari, who is definitely not what she seems. Now, Kazu and Akari must join forces to find and protect the source of the temple's power, before it's too late. Temple Alley Summer An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) business days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Author Talk with Kate Quinn (Virtual)
events.description: Register today to attend this program! Book Portals and Journeys of Literary Magic You’re invited to join us for a virtual conversation with acclaimed author Kate Quinn about her latest fantastical work, The Astral Library, which poses the question: Have you ever wished you could live inside a book? Welcome to the Astral Library, where books are not just objects, but doors to new worlds, new lives and new futures. This program is presented by Kate Quinn, a New York Times and USA Today bestselling author of historical fiction. A lifelong history buff, she has written four novels in the Empress of Rome Saga and two books set in the Italian Renaissance before turning to the 20th century with The Alice Network, The Huntress, The Rose Code, The Diamond Eye and The Briar Club.

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: Starter Villain
events.description: Join the Garfield Ridge Branch for a discussion of Starter Villain by John Scalzi "Inheriting your uncle's supervillain business is more complicated than you might think. Particularly when you discover who's running the place. Charlie's life is going nowhere fast. A divorced substitute teacher living with his cat in a house his siblings want to sell, all he wants is to open a pub downtown, if only the bank will approve his loan. Then his long-lost uncle Jake dies and leaves his supervillain business (complete with island volcano lair) to Charlie. But becoming a supervillain isn't all giant laser death rays and lava pits. Jake had enemies, and now they're coming after Charlie. His uncle might have been a stand-up, old-fashioned kind of villain, but these are the real thing: rich, soulless predators backed by multinational corporations and venture capital. It's up to Charlie to win the war his uncle started against a league of supervillains. But with unionized dolphins, hyper-intelligent talking spy cats, and a terrifying henchperson at his side, going bad is starting to look pretty good. In a dog-eat-dog world...be a cat" Accessibility Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Spicy Romance Book Club
events.description: Join us as we discuss romance books with lots of spice! This month's title is The Beloved by J.R. Ward. Must be 18 or older to join. Registration not required.

events.title: Books on Tap 20s and 30s Book Club
events.description: Join us on the second Thursday of each month at 7:00 p.m. for Matthews' 20s and 30s' book club! We will meet at a location in downtown Matthews (TBD). Register to be notified about our meeting location the week of the meeting. In March, we'll be discussing Kiley Reid's Come and Get It. This program will be provided by Matthews branch library staff. Information about this Group Books on Tap meets monthly to discuss everything from fiction to biographies, and we're open to all types of books! Here are the titles for the 2026 meetings. NOTE: Charlotte Mecklenburg Library is committed to public safety and continues to intentionally and deliberately expand in-person services that align with the COVID-19 safety guidelines set by Mecklenburg County, the Mecklenburg Board of County Commissioners (BOCC), the state of North Carolina and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC). For the safety of staff and other program participants, please stay home if you feel sick, are experiencing symptoms of COVID-19, have a confirmed case of COVID-19, are awaiting COVID-19 test results, or have recently been exposed to someone with COVID-19. Want to be notified about future events? Subscribe to our newsletter here.

location.details: At a bar or brewery in the Matthews area.

events.title: Book Portals and Journeys of Literary Magic with Kate Quinn
events.description: You’re invited to join us for a virtual conversation with acclaimed author Kate Quinn about her latest fantastical work, The Astral Library, which poses the question: Have you ever wished you could live inside a book? Welcome to the Astral Library, where books are not just objects, but doors to new worlds, new lives, and new futures. There is no need to register here but please do register at our partner site. Alexandria “Alix” Watson has learned one lesson from her barren childhood in the foster-care system: unlike people, books will never let you down. Working three dead-end jobs to make ends meet and knowing college is a pipe dream, Alix takes nightly refuge in the high-vaulted reading room at the Boston Public Library, escaping into her favorite fantasy novels and dreaming of far-off lands. Until the day she stumbles through a hidden door and meets the Librarian: the ageless, acerbic guardian of a hidden library where the desperate and the lost escape to new lives...inside their favorite books. The Librarian takes a dazzled Alix under her wing, but before she can escape into the pages of her new life, a shadowy enemy emerges to threaten everyone the Astral Library has ever helped protect. Aided by a dashing costume-shop owner, Alix and the Librarian flee through the Regency drawing rooms of Jane Austen to the back alleys of Sherlock Holmes and the champagne-soaked parties of The Great Gatsby as danger draws inexorably closer. But who does their enemy really wish to destroy—Alix, the Librarian, or the Library itself? Register now to hear more about The Astral Library, crafted for all bookworms and lovers of literature. About the Author: Kate Quinn is the New York Times and USA Today bestselling author of historical fiction. A native of Southern California, she attended Boston University, where she earned bachelor’s and master’s degrees in classical voice. A lifelong history buff, she has written four novels in the Empress of Rome Saga and two books set in the Italian Renaissance before turning to the 20th century with The Alice Network, The Huntress, The Rose Code, The Diamond Eye, and The Briar Club. The Astral Library is her first foray into magic realism. She and her husband now live in Maryland with their rescue dogs.

events.title: Virtual Author Talk: Kate Quinn
events.description: Step into a world where stories become real as we explore Kate Quinn’s imaginative new novel The Astral Library. The story follows Alexandria “Alix” Watson, who escapes her difficult past by losing herself in the Boston Public Library until she discovers a hidden doorway leading to a magical library where readers can enter the worlds they love. Guided by the sharp‑tongued Librarian, Alix soon finds herself pursued by a mysterious enemy and racing through the pages of classic literature, from Jane Austen to Sherlock Holmes to The Great Gatsby, as she fights to protect the Astral Library and the lives it shelters.This program is part of Santa Clara County Library District's celebration of Silicon Valley Reads. ---The Santa Clara County Library District is proud to present a free virtual author‑talk series featuring bestselling, award‑winning, and acclaimed writers from around the world, offering live Q&A sessions two to three times a month. Event links are sent about 24 hours in advance (or upon late registration), recordings are available for a limited time, and a full list of upcoming programs can be found here. ---For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org.

events.title: Out & About Reading Party
events.description: Join fellow book lovers from across Omaha for a quiet reading party with the Omaha Public Library! This nontraditional book club meets on the second Thursday of each month.This month we are at The Joslyn in the Fountain Court. This month we are at The Joslyn! Museum admission is free. Please review the museum guidelines before attending this event.There are no assigned readings—simply bring whatever you're currently reading, whether it's an ebook, audiobook, textbook, or comic book. It's truly "BYOBook." If you happen to forget your book or just pop in, OPL will have reading materials available for the duration of the program.Settle in for an hour of quiet, uninterrupted reading. Afterward, attendees have the option to socialize or not, as they prefer!Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please email Nancy Novotny at NNovotny@omahalibrary.org to request accommodation. Requests must be made at least five business days before the activity or event.

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: The Nickel Boys
events.description: Join the Walker Branch on the 2nd Thursday of each month at 6:30 pm for book discussions for adults. The March book selection is The Nickel Boys by Colson Whitehead. You may pick up a copy of the next title after each discussion at the Walker Branch circulation desk. AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Adult Contemporary Book Discussion: The Little Liar
events.description: Join the Lincoln Park Branch for a discussion of The Little Liar by Mitch Albom. "Eleven-year-old Nico Crispi never told a lie. When the Nazi's invade his home in Salonika, Greece, the trustworthy boy is discovered by a German officer, who offers him a chance to save his family. All Nico has to do is convince his fellow Jewish residents to board trains heading towards "the east" where they are promised jobs and safety. Unaware that this is all a cruel ruse, the innocent boy goes to the station platform every day and reassures the passengers that the journey is safe. But when the final train is at the station, Nico sees his family being loaded into a large boxcar crowded with other neighbors. Only after it is too late does Nico discover that he helped send the people he loved--and all the others--to their doom at Auschwitz. Nico never tells the truth again."--Copies of the title are available at the circulation desk. Registration is suggested. How to Attend This event takes place in person. Masks are strongly encouraged in all CPL locations. Questions about visiting the library? Check out our Using the Library FAQs. Accessibility Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.description: Join our discussion of interesting graphic novels and comic books. Bring along your recommendations for future meetings. Lending copies may be picked up at the information desk prior to meeting. March 12: Wonder Woman mix by various authors April 9: Spent by Alison Bechdel May 14: Ginseng Roots by Craig Thompson

events.title: Excelsior Book Club: Home Front
events.description: Join our discussion of interesting titles. The book club meets in-person on the second Thursday of each month. March 12: Home Front by Kristin Hannah April 9: The Serviceberry by Robin Wall KimmererMay 14: The Frozen River by Ariel Lawhon View complete book list from 2025-2026 discussed by Excelsior Book Club. Copies of the book will be available to check out from Excelsior Library while supplies last. Participants can also request a copy of the book (print, eBook and/or eAudiobook) through www.hclib.org. If you would like assistance to place a hold or are struggling to get the book for discussion, Ask Us Services will help expedite this for you.

events.title: Community Connections Book Club Discussion
events.description: Join our discussion of new and interesting titles focused on transforming perceptions and building a more equitable and connected community. A limited number of lending copies may be picked up at the information desk prior to each discussion. Upcoming titles include:February 12: The Sum of Us: What Racism Costs Everyone and How We Can Prosper Together by Heather C. McGheeMarch 12: The Color of Law, a Forgotten History of How Our Government Segregated America by Richard RothsteinApril 9: Mary Ann Key Book Club SelectionMay 14: Finding Your Third Place: Building Happier Communities (and Making Great Friends Along the Way) by Richard Kyte

book.title: The Color of Law, a Forgotten History of How Our Government Segregated America

events.title: Inclusive Book Club with AAC (Online Event)
events.description: Hello Book Club Families, Book of the Week: A to Z Mysteries: The Canary CaperC is for Canary . . . Green Lawn has a pet problem! Dink’s neighbor can’t find her canary. Ruth Rose’s cat, Tiger, has also disappeared. Then the kids hear about two more missing critters. Is there a pet-napper in town? Dink, Josh, and Ruth Rose are on the case! About Our Book Club: Each week, an AAC user hosts our sessions, and a librarian reads our story. After each reading, we enjoy an activity or game connected to the book, celebrating representation, creativity, and the many wonderful ways people communicate. We celebrate all forms of communication—AAC, speech, gestures, and expressions—and follow a Floortime-inspired approach that centers on meeting readers where they are and building joyful shared experiences. When applicable, a visual schedule will be emailed 24 hours before each session to help our readers follow along. Special thanks to Ron Roy and Penguin Random House for allowing us to read A to Z Mysteries: The Canary Caper in our virtual book club.

book.title: A to Z Mysteries: The Canary Caper

events.title: Kids' Evening Read Aloud
events.description: Pop on your pajamas, get comfy and listen to Ms. Mary from the West Town Branch read aloud the book Mrs. Piggle-Wiggle by Betty MacDonald over five Thursday evenings. You’ll receive an email with a link to the secure Zoom meeting about 24 hours before the meeting. Automatic transcription is included in all CPL events using Zoom. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event. Attending CPL Online EventsQuestions about attending online events? Check out our Events FAQs.

events.title: Halcyon Days Book Club: The Listeners
events.description: Halcyon Days is a book club that doesn't take itself too seriously; like hanging out with old friends you didn't know you had! Readers interested in exploring books and popular culture will find the group appealing. Hosted by Becky and Karen from Web and Online Services, this book club meets virtually on the second Thursday of each month. The link to the live online discussion will be emailed to registrants in advance (within 48 hours of the event). February 12: The Final Revival of Opal & Nev by Dawnie Walton March 12: The Listeners by Maggie Stiefvater April 9: The Serviceberry by Robin Wall Kimmerer May 14: Aftertaste by Daria Lavelle June 11: Seven Days in June by Tia Williams View complete book list for 2026 discussed by Halcyon Days. Participants can request a copy of the book (print, eBook and/or eAudiobook) through www.hclib.org. If you would like assistance to place a hold or are struggling to get the book for discussion, Ask Us Services will help expedite this for you. events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Fahrenheit 451
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Join us to share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. "Fahrenheit 451" by Ray BradburySYNOPSIS: Guy Montag is a fireman. His job is to destroy the most illegal of commodities, the printed book, along with the houses in which they are hidden. Montag never questions the destruction and ruin his actions produce, returning each day to his bland life and wife, Mildred, who spends all day with her television "family." But when he meets an eccentric young neighbor, Clarisse, who introduces him to a past where people didn't live in fear and to a present where one sees the world through the ideas in books instead of the mindless chatter of television, Montag begins to question everything he has ever known.

events.title: Point Reyes and Tomales Bay: A People’s History in Print
events.description: Point Reyes is known for its organic farms and food, its natural beauty preserved in public parks, its creative communities, and its Instagram-ready attractions. If one looks back in history, the area surrounding Tomales Bay is also notable for the resilience of its Indigenous community, for the creation of the western dairy industry and its technological innovations, for the 1960s rock stars who lived in or frequented the area, and as a superb living example of America’s melting pot, where immigrants settled to find better lives—and succeeded.Dewey’s new book, Point Reyes And Tomales Bay, chronicles the land and people of Point Reyes, Tomales Bay, and the towns that were and still are very different from each other: Olema, Marshall, Point Reyes Station, and Inverness.California historian Dewey Livingston has been fascinated with Marin history since high school, spending the past 55 years exploring and researching his home, especially West Marin. For decades a National Park Service historian specializing in the rural West, and ten years as a MCFL map archivist, he spent the past ten years on a comprehensive history of the Point Reyes area.Sponsored by Friends of the Fairfax Library. events.description: Monthly book discussion on a graphic novel selected by the group! This program is for readers age 16 and up. 2025-2026 titles: September 11: Understanding Comics by Scott McCloud (1994), 224 pagesOctober 9: Artificial: A Love Story by Amy Kurzweil (2023), 368 pagesNovember 13: The Road by Cormac McCarthy (2024), 160 pagesDecember 11: The House by Paco Roca (2019), 134 pagesJanuary 8: In: A Graphic Novel by Will McPhail (2021), 272 pagesFebruary 12: Special presentations by book club members who are authors: Mimi & Soni Discover Dussehra Legends by Devika Joglekar (2019), Kye in the Valley of Gold by Kyle Hurlbut (2024), Smiling Through the Mask by Daniele Archambault (2022), as well as artist Deborah J. Miller who will be sharing her current project.March 12: Feeding Ghosts by Tessa Hulls (2024), 400 pagesApril 9: Yellow Cab by Benoit Cohen (2022), 168 pagesMay 14: 40 Men and 12 Rifles: Indochina 1954 by Marcelino Truong (2023), 296 pagesTitles can be borrowed from the Palo Alto City Library and through our Link+ system - click on the links above or search in our catalog for more information. Meetings are held in person in the Midtown Room at Mitchell Park Library and facilitated by Danièle Archambault. The GNBC has been meeting since 2015! Danièle Archambault, Ph.D. (Linguistics), is a researcher, author, and educator who is interested in comics as a way to document a society's cultural and linguistic landscape. Originally from Montreal, Quebec, she now lives in Palo Alto, where she is an artist in residence at the Cubberley Artist Studio Program, a program of the City of Palo Alto. In the past few years, she has published several full-color comic books, graphic novels, and electronic books. Ms. Archambault is a regular guest speaker on documenting culture and dialects through comics at various educational and cultural institutions. She teaches Visual Storytelling at the Palo Alto Art Center, and at other educational venues. http://danielebd.com/

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Skylark
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read Sarah, Plain and Tall by Patricia MacLachlan. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Elaine Hilbelink. Meeting dates: February 27; March 6, 13, 20, 27; April 3 Meeting time: Fridays 10am - 11am Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Skylark description:The second book in the series that began with the Newbery Medal–winning Sarah, Plain and Tall by Patricia MacLachlan. Sarah came to the prairie from Maine to marry Papa. But that summer, a drought turned the land dry and brown. Fires swept across the fields and coyotes came to the well in search of water. So Sarah took Anna and Caleb back east, where they would be safe. Papa stayed behind. He would not leave his land. Maine was beautiful, but Anna missed home, and Papa. And as the weeks went by, she began to wonder what would happen if the rains never came. Would she and Caleb and Sarah and Papa ever be a family again?

events.title: Graphic Novel Book Club: Night Chef
events.description: Join other readers to discuss a different graphic novel each month. Ages 10-14. Registration required. --- This month we're reading Night Chef by Mika Song. "Ratatouille meets the magic of Studio Ghibli as a lonely raccoon who secretly lives inside a fine French restaurant and yearns to be a chef must venture into the woods for the first time to help a baby crow find its home. The Night Chef has never had a family— only a dream. She's spent years living inside the walls of a human restaurant, honing her cooking skills. She knows she can fry an omelette with the best of them— but she also knows that humans will never accept her. And so she keeps to herself, hidden and alone. Then, one evening, when Night Chef cracks an egg open for her dinner, a baby crow named Ichi falls out! Despite being reluctant to leave her home, Night Chef steps into the wilds in order to reunite Ichi with his family. And along the journey, she might just find a family to call her own, too."

events.title: 👨‍💼📝TAC (Teen Advisory Committee) 🧑‍⚖️👨‍💼🧑‍💻📜📝
events.description: Join the Teen Advisory Committee (TAC): Lead, Serve, and Create! Are you a teen in grades 7–12 looking to make a real impact in your community? The West University Branch Library is seeking motivated young leaders to join the Teen Advisory Committee (TAC). As a formal body of teen advocates, the TAC bridges the gap between Harris County youth, community, and library administration through service, advocacy, and leadership. Why Join TAC? Becoming a member is more than just joining a club; it is a professional leadership position that offers significant benefits for your future that may include: Earn Volunteer Hours: Receive official service hours for attending monthly meetings and assisting with library initiativesDevelop Core Skills: Build a competitive resume by gaining experience in project management, public speaking, and strategic planning.Exclusive Perks: Get "first dibs" on new Young Adult (YA) books and Manga arrivals, and receive a formal Letter of Recommendation upon successful completion of your termReal Influence: Manage a "micro-budget" to choose committee snacks or décor and help curate the library’s collection What You’ll Do As a TAC member, you are a civic consultant for the library. Your responsibilities may include: Event Planning: Brainstorming and hosting events like gaming tournaments, craft workshops, or "Comic-Con" task forcesAdvocacy: Suggesting new Manga or YA titles and ensuring teen spaces meet the needs of your peers.Outreach: Creating promotional content for social media and coordinating with local school clubs. How to Apply Ready to lead? Interested students must submit a formal membership application and complete a brief orientation interview with the Staff Liaison, Mike.

events.title: Cook by the Book Discussion: The Soup Book
events.description: Do you enjoy browsing through cookbooks? Do you love trying new dishes? Are you looking for recipe inspiration? For March's discussion, attendees will discuss The Soup Book.Browse the book and select a recipe or two to make at home. Serve it to your family and friends and ask them for honest feedback about the dish. What worked? What didn't work? Would you change anything about the recipe? events.title: Bite Sized Book Club- The Factory Witches of Lowell
events.description: Think you're too busy for a book club? Think again! Schlafly's "Bite-Sized Books" features diverse selections under 250 pages - a perfect fit for busy students, parents and professionals. Whether you're looking to try new genres, sample a different perspective or fit in a quick read during your kiddo's nap time, this club has you covered. Join us for our Saturday one-hour discussions every month. And if our current theme isn't for you, don't fret - check back in July for our next topic!This unit's focus is "Fantastical History", and March's selection is "The Factory Witches of Lowell" by C.S. Malerich (128 pages). This read has elements of fantasy and historical fiction, with LGBTQIA+ characters. Description - "Faced with abominable working conditions, unsympathetic owners, and hard-hearted managers, the mill girls of Lowell have had enough. They're going on strike, and they have a secret weapon on their side: a little witchcraft to ensure that no one leaves the picket line."

events.title: Teen Writing Workshop
events.description: Have you always dreamed of one day writing a book? Maybe you have a few essays or stories written up and want feedback from other writers. Or perhaps you want to improve your skills by doing fun creative writing exercises! If that sounds like a good time to you, drop by this writing club! Everyone is welcome - from beginners to experienced authors. This group is for teens only. In this workshop we will: -Read targeted pieces to generate ideas about theme, plot twists, and writing techniques. -Peer review each other's work. -Do fun writing exercises. -Encourage each other to conquer writer's block and gain confidence in sharing our writing. This workshop will take place on two Saturdays per month. Registration recommended but not required. Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven business days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Between the Sheets: A Romance Book Club
events.description: If you love talking tropes, subgenres, HEA versus HFN then this is the book club for you. Every month we'll read a different romance book and come together to discuss what we loved or hated. Books will range from closed door to explicit and explore a wide variety of subgenres. All are welcome! This month's book is Morbidly Yours by Ivy Fairbanks. Callum Flannelly must marry before 35 to inherit his family’s funeral business, a problem for a man who’d rather jump into an open grave than go on a date. Lark Thompson, a lively Texan animator new to Galway and still grieving her husband, is dismayed to find herself living next door to a funeral home—until she learns of Callum’s dilemma. Determined to help, she guides him into the dating world, certain love is for others, not her. But as their friendship deepens, so does an undeniable attraction. Lark brings color to Callum’s grey life, and Callum begins to thaw Lark’s frozen heart. They can pretend they’re not falling for each other—but they’d be dead wrong. Steam Level: 🔥🔥🔥🔥/5 Morbidly Yours DML welcomes everyone to our programs. If you have questions or need any accommodations, please contact us so we can help make the program a good experience for you. Check out our social story to familiarize yourself with the Miami Township Branch!

events.title: Castro Valley Reads Discussion Group
events.description: Join us for a discussion of the book selection for Castro Valley Reads! Our selection for 2026 is Advice for Future Corpses* (And those who love them) by Sallie Tisdale. From the sublime (the faint sound of Mozart as you take your last breath) to the ridiculous (lessons on how to close the sagging jaw of a corpse), Tisdale leads us through the peaks and troughs of death with a calm, wise, and humorous hand. Advice for Future Corpses is more than a how-to manual or a spiritual bible: it is a graceful compilation of honest and intimate anecdotes based on the deaths Tisdale has witnessed in her work and life, as well as stories from cultures, traditions, and literature around the world. Free copies of the book are available while supplies last. The free copies will be distributed beginning Sunday, February 1, at 2 pm at the CV Reads Kick-Off Event. The book is also available in the Library collection. Advice for Future Corpses* An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Oh, the Horror! Book Club: The Haunting of Hill House
events.description: Do you love strange, creepy and straight-up scary books? Then join our online horror book club led by librarians at Chicago Public Library across the city. This month, we will be reading The Haunting of Hill House by Shirley Jackson.First published in 1959, Shirley Jackson's The Haunting of Hill House has been hailed as a perfect work of unnerving terror. It is the story of four seekers who arrive at a notoriously unfriendly pile called Hill House: Dr. Montague, an occult scholar looking for solid evidence of a "haunting"; Theodora, his lighthearted assistant; Eleanor, a friendless, fragile young woman well acquainted with poltergeists; and Luke, the future heir of Hill House. At first, their stay seems destined to be merely a spooky encounter with inexplicable phenomena. But Hill House is gathering its powers--and soon it will choose one of them to make its own.The greatest haunted house story ever written, the inspiration for the 10-part Netflix series directed by Mike Flanagan and starring Michiel Huisman, Carla Gugino, and Timothy HuttonFuture Oh the Horror! selections:April: Witchcraft for Wayward Girls by Grady Hendrix (4/18 at 1p.m.)May: The Manor of Dreams by Christine Li (TBA) Accessibility Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: Hum
events.description: Join us for a discussion of Hum by Helen Phillips. "In a city addled by climate change and populated by intelligent robots called 'hums,' May loses her job to artificial intelligence. In a desperate bid to resolve her family's debt and secure their future for another few months, she becomes a guinea pig in an experiment that alters her face so it cannot be recognized by surveillance. Seeking some reprieve from her recent hardships and from her family's addiction to their devices, she splurges on passes that allow them three nights' respite inside the Botanical Garden. But her insistence that her son, daughter, and husband leave their devices at home proves far more fraught than she anticipated, and the lush beauty of the Botanical Garden is not the balm she hoped it would be" -- Provided by publisher.AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? events.description: In celebrations of Women's History month join in the discussion of This Bridge Called My Back: Writings by Radical Women of Color edited by Moraga, Cherríe and Anzaldúa, Gloria.Originally released in 1981, This Bridge Called My Back is a testimony to women of color feminism as it emerged in the last quarter of the twentieth century. Through personal essays, criticism, interviews, testimonials, poetry, and visual art, the collection explores, as coeditor Cherríe Moraga writes, “the complex confluence of identities—race, class, gender, and sexuality—systemic to women of color oppression and liberation.” *AmazonHow to Attend This event is available in person and online. Attend in person Little Village Branch, 2311 S Kedzie Attend online You’ll receive an email with a link to the secure a Zoom meeting about 24 hours before the meeting. events.description: Each month, this book club will discuss books in a variety of genres, from fantasy to contemporary fiction, memoirs to graphic novels, and more! We also welcome allies wanting to expand their bookish horizons. We meet on the first Saturday of the month, with a couple exceptions due to holidays. events.title: Better with a Beverage Book Discussion: Bad Feminist
events.description: Join the Richard M. Daley Branch for a discussion of Bad Feminist by Roxane Gay.In these funny and insightful essays, Roxane Gay takes us through the journey of her evolution as a woman (Sweet Valley High) of color (The Help) while also taking readers on a ride through culture of the last few years (Girls, Django in Chains) and commenting on the state of feminism today (abortion, Chris Brown). The portrait that emerges is not only one of an incredibly insightful woman continually growing to understand herself and our society, but also one of our culture.Relax, enjoy a beverage and read. Over the course of the month, read Bad Feminist and aim to try at least one beverage from a Chicago-based institution of your choice while you read. At the end of the month, we will meet on Zoom to discuss the book and any interesting beverages you tried. Feel free to join the discussion BYOB style.Copies of the title are available for checkout at the Richard M. Daley Branch. This event is hosted by Jacob from the Richard M. Daley Branch. How to AttendThis event takes place on Zoom. Register by the Friday, March 13th. Only one registration per household is needed. You’ll receive an email with a link to the secure Zoom meeting about 24 hours before the meeting. Automatic transcription is included in all CPL events using Zoom. AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. events.title: Castro Valley Reads Discussion Group
events.description: Join us for a discussion of the book selection for Castro Valley Reads! Our selection for 2026 is Advice for Future Corpses* (And those who love them) by Sallie Tisdale. From the sublime (the faint sound of Mozart as you take your last breath) to the ridiculous (lessons on how to close the sagging jaw of a corpse), Tisdale leads us through the peaks and troughs of death with a calm, wise, and humorous hand. Advice for Future Corpses is more than a how-to manual or a spiritual bible: it is a graceful compilation of honest and intimate anecdotes based on the deaths Tisdale has witnessed in her work and life, as well as stories from cultures, traditions, and literature around the world. Free copies of the book are available while supplies last. The free copies will be distributed beginning Sunday, February 1, at 2 pm at the CV Reads Kick-Off Event. The book is also available in the Library collection. Advice for Future Corpses* An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Born A Crime
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Join our book club for spirited discussions on a wide range of fiction and non-fiction. Each month we'll tackle a new title. Whether you loved it, loathed it, or left it unfinished, we want to hear your take. Come share your impressions and connect with fellow readers! This week we will be reading Born A Crime by Trevor Noah. "One of the comedy world's fastest-rising stars tells his wild coming of age story during the twilight of apartheid in South Africa and the tumultuous days of freedom that followed. Noah provides something deeper than traditional memorists: powerfully funny observations about how farcical political and social systems play out in our lives. Trevor Noah is the host of The Daily Show with Trevor Noah, where he gleefully provides America with its nightly dose of serrated satire. He is a light-footed but cutting observer of the relentless absurdities of politics, nationalism and race--and in particular the craziness of his own young life, which he's lived at the intersections of culture and history. In his first book, Noah tells his coming of age story with his larger-than-life mother during the last gasps of apartheid-era South Africa and the turbulent years that followed. Noah was born illegal--the son of a white, Dutch father and a black Xhosa mother, who had to pretend to be his nanny or his father's servant in the brief moments when the family came together. His brilliantly eccentric mother loomed over his life--a comically zealous Christian (they went to church six days a week and three times on Sunday), a savvy hustler who kept food on their table during rough times, and an aggressively involved, if often seriously misguided, parent who set Noah on his bumpy path to stardom. The stories Noah tells are sometimes dark, occasionally bizarre, frequently tender, and always hilarious--whether he's subsisting on caterpillars during months of extreme poverty or making comically pitiful attempts at teenage romance in a color-obsessed world; whether's he's being thrown into jail as the hapless fall guy for a crime he didn't commit or being thrown by his mother from a speeding car driven by murderous gangsters."

events.title: BeTWEEN the Pages
events.description: Join us in the extraordinary world of Graphic Novels with BeTWEEN the Pages, a book club for ages 8-12! This month's read will be How to Talk to your Succulent by Zoe Perisco! Registration Required to attend After you have registered, you may pick up your copy of the book at the front desk. Eleven-year-old Adara and her dad are moving from California to the cold and flat lands of Michigan . . . and it sucks. After Mom's recent passing, it seems way too soon. Talking to Dad has always been harder than with Mom, but now it's like walking on eggshells. And why did Dad bring so many of Mom's houseplants across the country? Her mother might have been called the Plant Whisperer back home, but Adara is beginning to wonder if there is something more to it. But when she meets a little succulent named Perle, she unlocks the same magical ability her mother had: talking to plants!But taking care of Perle isn't as easy as it seems — the environment isn't suited for her, and Adara doesn't know how to meet her needs. Kind of like the situation Adara herself is in. If only Perle could ask Adara for help . . . and Adara could ask her dad.

events.title: Strange New Worlds
events.description: Join us usually, but not always, on the third Monday of each month at 7:00 pm for the Strange New Worlds book club! This month we will be reading two novellas by Veronica Roth: When Among Crows and To Clutch a Razor. "Pain is Dymitr's calling. His family is one in a long line of hunters who sacrifice their souls to slay monsters. Now he's tasked with a deadly mission: find the legendary witch Baba Jaga. To reach her, Dymitr must ally with the ones he's sworn to kill. Pain is Ala's inheritance. A fear-eating zmora with little left to lose, Ala awaits death from the curse she carries. When Dymitr offers her a cure in exchange for her help, she has no choice but to agree. Together they must fight against time and the wrath of the Chicago underworld. But Dymitr's secrets--and his true motives--may be the thing that actually destroys them" "A funeral. A heist. A desperate mission. When Dymitr is called back to the old country for the empty night, a funeral rite intended to keep evil at bay, it's the perfect opportunity for him to get his hands on his family's most guarded relic--a book of curses that could satisfy the debt he owes legendary witch Baba Jaga. But first he'll have to survive a night with his dangerous, monster-hunting kin. As the sun sets, the line between enemies and allies becomes razor-thin, and Dymitr's new loyalties are pushed to their breaking point. Family gatherings can be brutal. Dymitr's might just be fatal." This program will be provided by Charlotte Mecklenburg Library staff and our wonderful Volunteer leader. Information about this Group The Strange New Worlds Book Club meets on the 3rd Monday of the month to discuss Science Fiction and Fantasy titles. How to Join an Online ProgramThis event will take place Zoom and in person. Please call 704-416-4000 for more information.Looking for more technology resources? Check out this list!

events.description: A book club dedicated to "Journey to the West," the classic Chinese fiction. This club is designed for children who are fans of the story and is conducted entirely in Mandarin Chinese. The event is organized by dedicated teen volunteers from Lynbrook High School.小朋友们，你们听说过《西游记》吗？ 这是中国最有名的神话故事之一，讲述了一段惊险又有趣的旅程。在这个故事里，有聪明勇敢的孙悟空、善良慈悲的唐僧、憨厚搞笑的猪八戒，还有忠诚可靠的沙和尚。他们一路上经历了许多危险和考验，但靠着智慧和团结，成功完成了任务。这次旅程不仅是他们追寻经书的故事，更是一场关于勇气、坚持和成长的冒险！更多有关 美猴王系列丛书. 免费的亲子中文活動. 座位数量有限. 门票将在故事时间开始前 30 分钟在询问处领取。 圖書館中文资源: 大人最新中文书, 中文電子書, 非小說類電子書 (Overdrive) 有歡迎大家一起來借閱電子書. Overdirve平台: 小朋友的中文電子書, 大朋友的中文電子小說, 大朋友的非小說類的電子書籍. Hoopla平台: 中文有聲書籍 美国残疾人法案(ADA)申请提供协助 5/25/2026

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Tom Lake
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Come and share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. This week we will be reading Tom Lake by Ann Patchett.In the spring of 2020, Lara's three daughters return to the family's orchard in Northern Michigan. While picking cherries, they beg their mother to tell them the story of Peter Duke, a famous actor with whom she shared both a stage and a romance years before at a theater company called Tom Lake. As Lara recalls the past, her daughters examine their own lives and relationship with their mother, and are forced to reconsider the world and everything they thought they knew.

events.title: Manchester Book Group
events.description: Pick up the next book club selection at the Manchester library service desk following the previous month's meeting on the 3rd Monday of the month. **NOTE Meetings in January and February each year are held on the FOURTH Monday of the month due to the Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Day and President's Day holidays This month we are discussing:What An Owl Knows by Jennifer Ackerman From the Publisher: From the author of The Genius of Birds and The Bird Way, a brilliant scientific investigation into owls—the most elusive of birds—and why they exert such a hold on human imaginationWith their forward gaze and quiet flight, owls are often a symbol of wisdom, knowledge, and foresight. But what does an owl really know? And what do we really know about owls? Some two hundred sixty species of owls exist today, and they reside on every continent except Antarctica, but they are far more difficult to find and study than other birds because they are cryptic, camouflaged, and mostly active at night. Though human fascination with owls goes back centuries, scientists have only recently begun to understand the complex nature of these extraordinary birds.

events.title: The Detection Club
events.description: Join us on the 3rd Tuesday of each month at 10:00 am on Zoom for The Detection Book Club! This month's selection is The Girl From Greenwich Street: A Novel of Hamilton, Burr, and America's First Murder Trial by Lauren Willig. From Booklist: "Willig's latest takes readers to 1799 New York to focus on the new nation's first murder trial, which involved some familiar and compelling historical figures. In the last days of the eighteenth century, beautiful young Elma Sands is found dead in a well after leaving her home to marry her beau, Levi Weeks. Suspicion quickly falls on Levi, a lodger at the residence of Elma's cousin Catherine and her husband. Levi's wealthy brother hires Aaron Burr and Brockholst Livingston to defend Levi, and once Alexander Hamilton gets wind of the high-profile murder trial, he volunteers his services, determined to not only clear Levi's name but also uncover the real killer. Amid the drama of the well-publicized trial, Hamilton and Burr clash and work to outmaneuver each other in the courtroom. Willig offers a gripping dramatization of the proceedings as well as a comprehensive historical note that delves into the real-life case, the primary sources, and titles about the sensational trial. A perfect tale for fans of courtroom dramas and for lovers of historical fiction." This program will be provided by Davidson library staff. Information about this Group The Detection Book Club meets monthly to discuss Mystery and Suspense titles. For a list of all 2026 selections, please click here: Detection Book Club How to Join an Online ProgramThis event takes place on Zoom. Please register by 8:00 am the day of the event. You’ll receive an email with a secure Zoom link with a reminder about the meeting.Looking for more technology resources? Check out this list!
