events.title: Melinda Myers Webinar: Getting Started Growing Vegetable and Flower Gardens
events.description: Starting your first or even your second garden can be overwhelming. Melinda will cover the basics to help you get off to a great start this garden season. There will be tips on designing the garden, preparing the soil, as well as planting seeds and plants. As always, there will be time at the end of the webinar to answer your questions. Register for this FREE webinar at: https://www.melindamyers.com/about-melinda/upcoming-appearances*Sponsored by: Ebert's Greenhouse Village About Melinda MyersMelinda Myers has written more than 20 gardening books, including the Midwest Gardener’s Handbook, 2nd Edition and Small Space Gardening. She hosts The Great Courses “How to Grow Anything” streaming courses and the nationally syndicated Melinda’s Garden Moment radio program. Myers is also a columnist and contributing editor for Birds & Blooms magazine and writes and nationally syndicated weekly garden column. Before her current media work, Melinda hosted “The Plant Doctor” radio show on WTMJ 620 AM for over 20 years as well as 7 seasons of “Great Lakes Gardener” on PBS. She has a master’s degree in horticulture and was a horticulture instructor with tenure. She was also recently inducted into the National Association for Garden Communicators Hall of Fame.Melinda’s website is www.MelindaMyers.com where you can find gardening videos, gardening tips and information, monthly garden checklists, and more.

events.description: This themed, literacy-based drop in program is chock full of activities to help prepare your child for school and beyond. Manage your event registration through "My Events" when signed in to a library account. DISCOVER MORE Running of out books to read? events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Skylark
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read Sarah, Plain and Tall by Patricia MacLachlan. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Elaine Hilbelink. Meeting dates: February 27; March 6, 13, 20, 27; April 3 Meeting time: Fridays 10am - 11am Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Skylark description:The second book in the series that began with the Newbery Medal–winning Sarah, Plain and Tall by Patricia MacLachlan. Sarah came to the prairie from Maine to marry Papa. But that summer, a drought turned the land dry and brown. Fires swept across the fields and coyotes came to the well in search of water. So Sarah took Anna and Caleb back east, where they would be safe. Papa stayed behind. He would not leave his land. Maine was beautiful, but Anna missed home, and Papa. And as the weeks went by, she began to wonder what would happen if the rains never came. Would she and Caleb and Sarah and Papa ever be a family again?

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - The Game
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read The Game by Monica Hughes. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Rianne Hastie Adams. Meeting dates: February 27; March 6, 20, 27; April 3, 17. (No class March 13 and April 10.)Meeting time: Fridays 11:30am - 12:30pm Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. The Game description:It’s the year 2154. Lisse and her friends have been deemed unemployable in the eyes of society. Now they must scavenge the disintegrating city for food and shelter, just to make ends meet. But their dismal existence starts to look up when Lisse and her friends are invited to participate in The Game, a virtual reality experience where they are challenged to survive. But over time, the line between reality and fantasy starts to blur. What started as a simple exercise quickly becomes a test of endurance, trust, and their will to live.

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Ducks
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Come and share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. This week we will be reading Ducks by Kate Beaton Katie heads out west to take advantage of Alberta's oil rush-part of the long tradition of East Coasters who seek gainful employment elsewhere when they can't find it in the homeland they love so much. Katie encounters the harsh reality of life in the oil sands, where trauma is an everyday occurrence yet is never discussed. Beaton's natural cartooning prowess is on full display as she draws colossal machinery and mammoth vehicles set against a sublime Albertan backdrop of wildlife, northern lights, and boreal forest. Her first full length graphic narrative, Ducks: Two Years in the Oil Sands is an untold story of Canada: a country that prides itself on its egalitarian ethos and natural beauty while simultaneously exploiting both the riches of its land and the humanity of its people.

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: The Dutch House
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Join our book club for spirited discussions on a wide range of fiction and non-fiction. Each month we'll tackle a new title. Whether you loved it, loathed it, or left it unfinished, we want to hear your take. Come share your impressions and connect with fellow readers! This month we will be discussing The Dutch House by Ann Patchett. At the end of the Second World War, Cyril Conroy combines luck and a single canny investment to begin an enormous real estate empire, propelling his family from poverty to enormous wealth. His first order of business is to buy a lavish estate in the suburbs outside of Philadelphia. Meant as a surprise for his wife, the house sets in motion the undoing of everyone he loves. The story is told by Cyril's son Danny, as he and his older sister, the brilliantly acerbic and self-assured Maeve, are exiled from the house where they grew up by their stepmother. The two wealthy siblings are thrown back into the poverty their parents had escaped from and find that all they have to count on is one another. It is this unshakeable bond between them that both saves their lives and thwarts their futures.

events.description: Welcome to the Borderlands Reads Book Club! This is a monthly book club with a lively discussion for adults! We are a community book club celebrating Borderlands literature featuring local and Indigenous authors, perspectives of the US-Mexico border, and stories rooted in the Sonoran Desert, inviting all to explore. We meet in person on the third Saturday of the month, from 10 to 11:30 a.m. Registration is not required. Here is the list of titles we will read in 2026 (selection and sequencing subject to availability and the whims of the club leader). There is a monthly email newsletter with reminders and updates. The book for our March meeting is The Bean Trees by Barbara Kingsolver, who just won the Pulitzer Prize and lived in Tucson. events.title: Murphy-Wilmot Book Club
events.description: Join us to discuss what's hot in fiction and non-fiction. New members are always welcome to drop in on this informal gathering of book lovers. This month, we are reading Lydia Millet's latest short story collection, Atavists. A collection of stories that follows a bewildered bartender, a grieving beautician, a jilted urban planner and others as their lives unravel in unexpected ways, revealing primal urges and raw emotions against the backdrop of a fractured, overwhelmed America.“With her sharply honed perspective on our digital bewitchment and destruction of nature, her shredding wit and depthless compassion, Millet deftly portrays individuals of different generations caught in tech-sparked predicaments absurd, heartbreaking, and enraging. These thought-provoking, surprising, charming, and deeply moving stories illuminate who we are at our core even as our lives are mediated by social media, dating apps, and surveillance cameras, even as the living world is being driven precipitously toward extinction."—Booklist (starred review)

events.title: Club Book with Lee Hawkins
events.description: Pulitzer Prize finalist Lee Hawkins is an acclaimed journalist, memoirist, and podcaster. Over nearly two decades with The Wall Street Journal, Hawkins honed his skill and voice as an investigative journalist, news editor, and on-camera reporter. He gained a following with The Business of Celebrity with Lee Hawkins, a recurring series with Hollywood superstars, sports icons, and business legends that explored the entrepreneurial and financial aspects of fame. Later in his career, Hawkins pivoted to coverage of race, equity, and education, with strong emphasis on the enduring impacts of slavery and Jim Crow segregation. These are subjects with deep personal resonance for him. Hawkins explores centuries of oppression and trauma in his own lineage in I Am Nobody’s Slave: How Uncovering My Family’s History Set Me Free. I Am Nobody’s Slave is a potent blend of memoir, history, social commentary, investigative reporting, and cutting-edge genealogical science. In a glowing review, Forbes summarizes: “Hawkins confronts the lingering effects of intergenerational trauma, showing that healing is an act of defiance in itself.” All events are FREE, open to the public, and registration is not required. You can find 180+ podcasts and videos from past seasons as well as author info and a calendar of events at clubbook.org.Hosted in partnership with Club Book, a program of the Metropolitan Library Service Agency (MELSA) and is coordinated by Library Strategies, the nonprofit consulting group of The Friends of the St. Paul Public Library. The program is made possible through Minnesota’s Arts & Cultural Heritage Fund. Club Book is a unique player on the Minnesota literary events scene. They collaborate with all eight library systems in the seven-county region to pair bestselling and award-winning authors with audiences in all corners of the Twin Cities metro.

events.description: Noname Book Club is a Black-owned worker cooperative connecting community members both inside and outside carceral facilities with radical books. Each month, we uplift two books written by Black, indigenous, and other people of color. We believe building community through political education is crucial for our liberation. We also believe everyone (especially racialized and colonized people) should have access to unlimited educational materials. This is why we make sure all the resources we offer are free. Stay tuned for our next book club monthly selections! Get accessibility information.

Meeting datesJanuary 12, 26 (No class Jan 19)February 2, 23 (No class Feb 9, 16)March 2, 9, 23, 30 (No class Mar 16)April 13, 20, 27 (No class Apr 6)Meeting time: 10:15am - 11:45amWhere: In the library - your book club leader will tell you which tables. The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1 description:On a distant planet, a team of scientists are conducting surface tests, shadowed by their Company-supplied ‘droid ― a self-aware SecUnit that has hacked its own governor module, and refers to itself (though never out loud) as “Murderbot.” Scornful of humans, all it really wants is to be left alone long enough to figure out who it is (and to watch its favorite show in its downtime.) But when a neighboring mission goes dark, it’s up to the scientists and Murderbot to get to the truth.

events.title: BYOB(ook) Club
events.description: The book club where you choose your own book! Join us as we discuss our monthly theme and the unique book each person has chosen to read. This month's theme: Fresh Starts and Second Chances (If you're looking for the other type of BYOB, try our Boozy Book Club!)

events.title: ESL Book Club (Waitlist Available)
events.description: Let's read books and learn English at the same time. Join other language learners in a virtual meeting where we read together and discuss the text. Please be aware that this program has limited space. We'll read aloud and discuss the book The Boys in the Boat (Young Readers Adaptation) by Daniel James Brown. REGISTER HERE (Please note that if the Book Club is marked as FULL, you can still fill out this form to be added to the waitlist.)

events.title: Mood Readers Book Club
events.description: For those who are looking to chat about books and get recommendations, this club is for you! Join OPL on the last Monday of the month for a lively discussion about the books you’re currently reading or have read recently. Participants are encouraged to read something within the month's featured mood or theme. March's theme is Dreams. See some recommended titles. OPL Book Club discussions are led by OPL staff and are open to everyone. All Omaha Public Library locations are wheelchair accessible. Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please email Nancy Novotny at NNovotny@omahalibrary.org to request accommodation. Requests must be made at least five business days before the activity or event.

events.title: 星期一下午:西游记 (读书会)
events.description: A book club dedicated to "Journey to the West," the classic Chinese fiction. This club is designed for children who are fans of the story and is conducted entirely in Mandarin Chinese. The event is organized by dedicated teen volunteers from Lynbrook High School.小朋友们，你们听说过《西游记》吗？ 这是中国最有名的神话故事之一，讲述了一段惊险又有趣的旅程。在这个故事里，有聪明勇敢的孙悟空、善良慈悲的唐僧、憨厚搞笑的猪八戒，还有忠诚可靠的沙和尚。他们一路上经历了许多危险和考验，但靠着智慧和团结，成功完成了任务。这次旅程不仅是他们追寻经书的故事，更是一场关于勇气、坚持和成长的冒险！更多有关 美猴王系列丛书. 免费的亲子中文活動. 座位数量有限. 门票将在故事时间开始前 30 分钟在询问处领取。 圖書館中文资源: 大人最新中文书, 中文電子書, 非小說類電子書 (Overdrive) 有歡迎大家一起來借閱電子書. Overdirve平台: 小朋友的中文電子書, 大朋友的中文電子小說, 大朋友的非小說類的電子書籍. Hoopla平台: 中文有聲書籍 美国残疾人法案(ADA)申请提供协助 5/25/2026

events.title: Happy Ever After Book Club
events.description: Program will take place online via Google Meets. Join library staff to discuss selected romance titles! Winter 2026 titles: January 26 - Collide by Bal Khabra February 23 - Behooved by M. Stevenson March 30 - Savor It by Tarah DeWitt Join the Happy Ever After Facebook group or email cbrown@rdpl.org for the meeting link. New members are always welcome!

events.title: Seniors Social
events.description: Join us for a relaxed and welcoming Seniors Social designed to support activity, connection, and community. This free program offers a mix of activity, games, and social time in a friendly, inclusive space. Participants can enjoy a variety of activities, games, and sometimes guests, or simply sit back with a coffee or tea and connect with others. We’ll also feature large-print books and recipes for easy browsing. All abilities are welcome, and activities are optional. Come for what interests you and stay for what feels good.

events.title: Snacks and Stories
events.library: Public Library of Youngstown and Mahoning County
events.description: Join our teen book club meeting on the last Tuesday of the month in the Newport library teen room. Enjoy some provided snacks and talk about any of the books, audiobooks, and graphic novels or manga that you're reading.

events.title: StoryKids Reading Club (6–10 yrs)
events.description: Ages: 6–10 years Are you an avid reader? A bookworm? Register for our reading club, pick up this month's title, and meet friends in our newest reading club! We'll read a different book each month and gather at the library to talk about what we liked, didn't like, and more. We'll also do book-related activities. In March, we'll read Matilda by Roald Dahl and in April, we'll read Future Hero: Race to Fire Mountain by Remi Blackwood. Registration is required for the whole series.

events.title: Off the Shelves Book Club
events.library: Public Library of Youngstown and Mahoning County
events.description: Welcome to our monthly book club for a lively discussion of titles chosen "Off the Shelves" of the Canfield Library's Book Club room. We meet the last Tuesday of each month. March's book is The Emperor of Gladness by Ocean Vuong. Reserve your copy from the library's catalog today.

events.title: Richmond Green Evening Book Club
events.description: Looking for your next favourite read? Join our new Richmond Green evening book club to read and discuss great titles! New members welcome. In March, we'll read In the Time of Our History by Susanne Pari and in April, we'll read Run Towards the Danger by Sarah Polley. Note: Please register for each month separately.

events.title: Historical Fiction Book Club
events.description: Step back in time and explore the past through compelling stories and unforgettable characters. During our monthly Historical Fiction Book Club, we discuss new books, time periods, and perspectives. All are welcome—whether you're a history buff or new to the genre. Stop by the main library to check out the current title, while supplies last. Digital and audio versions are also available on either Hoopla or Libby. This book club meets on the last Tuesday of the month. If you need an accessibility accommodation to participate, please feel free to let us know. Here’s our Spring lineup: March - The Four Winds by Kristin Hannah April - In the Time of the Butterflies by Julia Alvarez May - The Christie Affair by Nina de Garmont

events.title: Next Chapter: The Reading Room
events.description: Bridge the gap between teen and adult programming at the Moore Library every Tuesday for our New Adult event series! Geared roughly towards ages 18-25. With different activities every week, build community with your neighbors as you explore different crafts and hobbies. All supplies provided! This week, come join in a low stakes book club! Bring your own book or browse our collection for a quick read. Find a spot to relax and read, and discuss your favorites with your neighbors.

events.title: Fated Pages Book Club
events.description: Love romance with a touch of magic? Fated Pages is a monthly book club dedicated to romantasy—where love stories unfold in worlds filled with magic, adventure, and mythical creatures. Join fellow readers as we dive into captivating tales of romance and fantasy. We meet at a.casa (3723 S Kingshighway Blvd, St. Louis, MO 63109) on the last Tuesday of each month at 6:30 PM. The event is free! Guests are encouraged to order food and drink from a.casa, but no purchases are necessary for participation. Food and drinks may not be available, depending on vendor availability. Whether you're new to the genre or a seasoned romantasy fan, all are welcome! This month's feature is: The Jasad Heir by Sara Hashem. You can pick up a physical copy of this month's title from the second-floor desk at Buder Library or check it out digitally as an ebook or audiobook.

book.title: The Jasad Heir by Sara Hashem

events.description: Come indulge in a vibrant book club that sparks connections through engaging discussions and a shared love for young adult fiction. This book club is for all over the age of 14. book.title: One of Us Is Lying by Karen M. McManus

events.title: Martinez Library Book Club
events.description: Join us for a lively discussion of a classic book. Ask at the service desk to check out a copy of this month's title. March's book is: Silas Marner, by George Eliot.

events.description: PLEASE NOTE: This month's meeting has been rescheduled from March 24th to March 31st, thank you for your patience!The Kensington Book Club is open to all and alternates between classic and contemporary literature on a monthly basis. The club meets online via Zoom, usually meets on the fourth Tuesday at 6:15pm. March's book is The Sound of Things Falling by Juan Gabriel Vasquez. For details on how to join the Zoom meetings, please contact the Kensington Library.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - One Foot in the Grape
events.description: Carla's intermediate ESL Book Club will read One Food in the Grap by Carlene O'Neil. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Carla Passero. Meeting dates: January 13, 20, 27; February 3, 10, 17, 24; March 3, 24, 31. (No class March 10 or March 17).Meeting time: Tuesdays, 7:00pm - 8:00pmWhere: CCC Room next to Member Services DeskRegistration is open now! Book club is limited to 8 participants. Books will be provided at the first meeting for registered participants to keep, If you do not show up at the first meeting and have not made arrangements with the tutor, we will give your spot to the next learner on the wait list. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. About the book:One Foot in the Grape is a cozy mystery about ex-photojournalist Penny Lively, who inherits a California winery, only to get tangled in murder when her neighbor's wine is sabotaged and a staff member ends up dead in a grape crusher, making her niece the prime suspect, forcing Penny to solve the crime before her family is ruined. It's the first in the Cypress Cove Mystery series, blending wine country charm with amateur sleuthing and danger.

events.title: Books on Tap
events.description: Join us at Books on Tap to discuss Murderland, by Caroline Fraser.What makes a murderer? Pulitzer winner Fraser (Prairie Fires) makes a convincing case for arsenic and lead poisoning as contributing factors in this eyebrow-raising account. Fraser, who was born and raised in the Pacific Northwest, where smelters belted out poison for decades and a proliferation of serial killers in the 1970s and '80s earned the region the nickname "America's Killing Fields," marries a poignant memoir of her Washington State childhood with a vivid catalog of crimes by Ted Bundy, the Green River Killer, and others. The eBook and eAudiobook are available on Libby. This month's location will be The Parkway Tavern MurderlandExplore all of Tacoma Public Library's Book Clubs.

events.description: Join us on Wednesday afternoons for a creative adventure. These craft sessions focus on having fun and exploring new crafts, rather than striving for perfection. These can be used to keep your favourite book, notebook, eReader, or small tablet protected. We'll start with a base of coloured felt, and decorate them using the templates provided or with your own unique designs. Just bring your enthusiasm and enjoy creating in a laid-back environment.

events.description: Join the San Carlos Library’s fiction book club as we explore world literature one translation at a time. Each month, we will dive into a translated book originally written in a language other than English, with a special focus on works from the 21st century. Our selections will highlight diverse cultures and fresh literary voices from around the globe. To accommodate different schedules, this book club will meet twice a month - on the first Monday of the month at 6 PM and on the first Wednesday of the month at 10:30 AM. Stop by on the day that works for you to join the discussion!For April, we will be reading and discussing A General Theory of Oblivion by Jose Eduardo Agualusa.

events.title: Melinda Myers Webinar: Growing a Bountiful Harvest
events.description: Learn some tips and strategies that will help you to maximize your harvest in any available space. Whether you have a dedicated garden, grow in containers or squeeze fruits, vegetables, and herbs into your other garden beds, we will help you grow lots of produce no matter what your limitations. We will discuss plant varieties as well as planting and harvesting techniques to help increase your vegetable gardening success! Register for this FREE webinar at: https://www.melindamyers.com/about-melinda/upcoming-appearances *The webinar is sponsored by: About Melinda MyersMelinda Myers has written more than 20 gardening books, including the Midwest Gardener’s Handbook, 2nd Edition and Small Space Gardening. She hosts The Great Courses “How to Grow Anything” streaming courses and the nationally syndicated Melinda’s Garden Moment radio program. Myers is also a columnist and contributing editor for Birds & Blooms magazine and writes and nationally syndicated weekly garden column. Before her current media work, Melinda hosted “The Plant Doctor” radio show on WTMJ 620 AM for over 20 years as well as 7 seasons of “Great Lakes Gardener” on PBS. She has a master’s degree in horticulture and was a horticulture instructor with tenure. She was also recently inducted into the National Association for Garden Communicators Hall of Fame.Melinda’s website is www.MelindaMyers.com where you can find gardening videos, gardening tips and information, monthly garden checklists, and more.

events.description: Fourth graders are invited to join us for Fantastic Fourth Grade Book Club! Book Club will be meeting on the first Wednesday of the month from 5:00-5:45 pm. This group is limited to 12 students and does require registration. Copies of the book will be available for check out at the Information Desk after each book club meeting. Please remember to bring your Alameda County Library card (or the card number) with you in order to check out the book for next month's meeting. Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests! An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) days in advance. events.title: Science Fiction & Fantasy Book Club
events.description: This month, the group will be discussing A Declaration of the Rights of Magicians, by H. G. Parry. Join us for a virtual book group meeting for fans of speculative fiction! To participate, click on the REGISTER button on this page, and include your email address. Once you have registered here, you will be sent the meeting ID and password. You do not have to appear via video if you don't want to - you can join using audio and chat only, if that is your preference. At the scheduled meeting time, click on the link you have been sent to join.

events.title: Silent Book Club
events.description: Do you find it difficult to find time to read? Do you hate reading assigned books? Are you anxious in a traditional book club setting? If you answered yes to any of these, then check out Silent Book Club. A new type of book club, where there's no assigned reading and all readers are welcome.BYOBook (print, eBooks, audiobooks, textbooks, comic books, etc.) and settle in for an hour of silent, sustained reading. At the end of the hour, attendees can discuss what they're reading—or not! A fun way to hear about what others are reading and share what you're reading. Best of all, there's dedicated time each month to read something that interests you. Silent Book Club meets the first Wednesday of the month in Creativity Commons on Level 1. Any person needing an accommodation for a disability to access the benefits of the Library’s services, programs, or activities under the Americans with Disabilities Act should contact the Library by telephone at (972) 292-5669, by email at library@friscotexas.gov, or in writing, not less than five (5) business days prior to the program.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Chains (The Seeds of America Trilogy)
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read Chains (The Seeds of America Trilogy) by Laurie Halse Anderson. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Roberta Linsky. Meeting dates: February 26; March 5, 12, 19; April 2, 16; May 7. (No class March 26; April 9, 23, 30.)Meeting time: Thursdays from 10:00am - 11:00amWhere: Tables by the wall posters Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Book description:As the Revolutionary War begins, thirteen-year-old Isabel wages her own fight...for freedom. Promised freedom upon the death of their owner, she and her sister, Ruth, in a cruel twist of fate become the property of a malicious New York City couple, the Locktons, who have no sympathy for the American Revolution and even less for Ruth and Isabel. When Isabel meets Curzon, a slave with ties to the Patriots, he encourages her to spy on her owners, who know details of British plans for invasion. She is reluctant at first, but when the unthinkable happens to Ruth, Isabel realizes her loyalty belongs to whoever can provide her with freedom.

events.title: Making Stories
events.description: Children ages 8 to 12 are invited to join a club that meets on the first Thursday of each month from 3:30-5 pm to write, illustrate, make, and tell your own stories. This is a workshop, where kids can work alone, in pairs, or in small groups to write and illustrate stories. Follow a prompt, do a fun writing or drawing exercise, or free write. Create a book, a zine, a comic, a story, a kamishibai, a play, or whatever! We'll set a date for people who want to read aloud their finished stories and celebrate, and we welcome stories to be added into the library collection. Drop in, no registration required. New members always welcome. Please note: In January, we'll meet on the 2nd Thursday. Get accessibility information.

events.title: Kids Culture Club
events.description: Embark on a global adventure by attending Culture Club on the first Thursday of every month! In each session, young explorers will learn more about an important culture through an engaging activity, such as a craft, game, and/or books and audiovisual media. From making traditional art to viewing a new film, each gathering is a fun-filled journey that fosters curiosity and appreciation for the world’s rich diversity. Perfect for ages 6-11, Culture Club invites kids to connect, create, and celebrate together—one culture at a time! No registration required and all materials will be provided.This month, we're exploring Greek history, culture, and heritage by making scratch art in the shape of Greek vases!

We will meet in the Reading Garden. In case of rain, we will meet instead in the Twain meeting room. Registration not required but if you sign up, you'll get a reminder prior to the event!

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Skylark
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read Sarah, Plain and Tall by Patricia MacLachlan. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Elaine Hilbelink. Meeting dates: February 27; March 6, 13, 20, 27; April 3 Meeting time: Fridays 10am - 11am Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Skylark description:The second book in the series that began with the Newbery Medal–winning Sarah, Plain and Tall by Patricia MacLachlan. Sarah came to the prairie from Maine to marry Papa. But that summer, a drought turned the land dry and brown. Fires swept across the fields and coyotes came to the well in search of water. So Sarah took Anna and Caleb back east, where they would be safe. Papa stayed behind. He would not leave his land. Maine was beautiful, but Anna missed home, and Papa. And as the weeks went by, she began to wonder what would happen if the rains never came. Would she and Caleb and Sarah and Papa ever be a family again?

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - The Game
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read The Game by Monica Hughes. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Rianne Hastie Adams. Meeting dates: February 27; March 6, 20, 27; April 3, 17. (No class March 13 and April 10.)Meeting time: Fridays 11:30am - 12:30pm Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. The Game description:It’s the year 2154. Lisse and her friends have been deemed unemployable in the eyes of society. Now they must scavenge the disintegrating city for food and shelter, just to make ends meet. But their dismal existence starts to look up when Lisse and her friends are invited to participate in The Game, a virtual reality experience where they are challenged to survive. But over time, the line between reality and fantasy starts to blur. What started as a simple exercise quickly becomes a test of endurance, trust, and their will to live.

events.title: Adv. 3D Printing for Youth
events.description: Ready to take your 3D design skills to the next level? Join us at Advanced 3D Printing for Youth! In this club, we’ll dive deeper into 3D modeling techniques, troubleshoot designs, and experiment with more advanced tools. Whether you want to create functional gadgets, detailed miniatures, or creative prints, this is your chance to push your understanding of 3D printing! EPL staff will provide guidance and feedback as you refine your designs! Each participant’s final design will be 3D printed for free and available for pickup at your local EPL branch. Recommended for ages 9-15 with prior experience in TinkerCAD or 3D design. If attendees want to create an object larger than 6cm x 6cm x 6cm, parents must complete the certification and bring a USB to the class so that the Makerspace can support with processing the print! To 3D print at the Makerspace: An adult (or youth aged 12+) must be certified to 3D print, which is a quick online process found on our website!Book the printer by calling 780-944-5342, email makerspace@epl.ca, or chat face to face with Makerspace staff!Slice the design in the Makerspace, in Children's library, in our class, or on your own devices!Bring the file on a USB to the Makerspace to process. Other branches with on-the-floor 3D printing services are: Abbottsfield, Mill Woods, and Capilano!

We also welcome allies wanting to expand their bookish horizons.2026 Book Schedule (subject to change): Jan 3 - Lessons in Magic and DisasterFeb 7 - Chain-Gang All-StarsMar 7 - Forest EuphoriaApr 4 - Is This a Cry for Help?May 2 - AtmosphereJun 6 - The Picture of Dorian GrayJul 11 - The EverlastingAug 1 - The UnworthySep 5 - Read a Queer Romance💖Oct 3 - On Earth We’re Briefly GorgeousNov 7 - Bury Our Bones in the Midnight SoilDec 5 - Holiday Special! events.title: Text & Textiles
events.description: A book club for crafty people! Do you like to read? Do you also like to create textiles? Let's combine the two into a new (crafty) book club we call Text & Textiles! Here's how it works: Read any book you want. Audiobooks count, too!Make a granny square, quilt block, or other textile in the colors found on the cover of the book you read.Bring your finished textile to the meeting, and tell us about the book you read. Over time, you'll have enough granny squares or quilt blocks to make a larger finished project. This program takes place in The Spark and is for adults. Registration is not required, but space is limited to the first 15 people. (Note: this session repeats on December 6. The same information will be covered. There is no need to attend both.) Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests!An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least 7 business days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Point Reyes Book Club: To The Bright Edge of the World
events.description: The Point Reyes Library Book Club will discuss To the Bright Edge of the World by Eowyn Ivey on Monday, April 6 from 7 to 8 pm. This book club meeting is online. Facilitator - Lorelei Sontag Contact: Mattie Ivy Leeds at 415-663-8375 or mattie.ivyleeds@marincounty.gov to register, or for more information. About the Book: To the Bright Edge of the World by Eowyn IveyIn the winter of 1885, decorated war hero Colonel Allen Forrester leads an exploratory expedition up the Wolverine River and into the vast, untamed Alaska Territory. Leaving behind Sophie, his newly pregnant wife, Forrester records his extraordinary experiences in hopes that his journal will reach her if he doesn't return. As they map the territory and gather information on native tribes, whose understanding of the natural world is unlike anything they have ever encountered, Forrester and his team can't escape the sense that some great, mysterious force threatens their lives. Meanwhile, in Vancouver, Sophie chafes under the social restrictions of a pregnant woman on her own, and yearns to travel alongside her husband. She, too, explores nature, through the new art of photography, unaware that the coming winter will test her own courage and faith to the breaking point. ADA Accommodation Request | Solicitud de Adaptación ADA

Participants can enjoy a variety of activities, games, and sometimes guests, or simply sit back with a coffee or tea and connect with others. We’ll also feature large-print books and recipes for easy browsing. All abilities are welcome, and activities are optional. Come for what interests you and stay for what feels good.

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Ridgerunner
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Come and share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. This month we will be reading: Ridgerunner by Gil AdamsonIn November, 1917, William Moreland is in mid-flight. After twenty years, the infamous thief, known as the Ridgerunner, has returned. Moving through the Rocky Mountains and across the border to Montana, the drifter, impoverished and aged beyond his years, is also a widower and a father. And he is determined to steal enough money to secure his son's future. Twelve-year-old Jack Boulton was born in the woods to two outlaws, but now finds himself semi-orphaned and left in the care of Sister Beatrice, a formidable nun of the Anglican Order of Saint Mara. In Banff, Alberta, where tourists, new immigrants, and POWs dwell among the locals, she lays claim to the boy and keeps him in cloistered seclusion in her grand old home. The boy longs to return to his family's cabin, deep in the Sawback Range. His father is coming for him. The nun won't let him go. Set against the backdrop of a distant war raging in Europe and a rapidly changing landscape in the West, Gil Adamson presents a literary Western brimming with a cast of unforgettable characters touched with humour and loss, and steeped in the wild of the natural world.

events.title: Mystery Book Club
events.description: Meet with your neighbors and fellow mystery lovers on the first Tuesday night of each month for a lively discussion of classic and current mysteries. Read this month's selection, drop in and join the fun! For April 7 the club will be discussing Drive your Plow Over the Bones of the Dead by Olga Tokarczuk. SynopsisIn a remote Polish village, Janina devotes the dark winter days to studying astrology, translating the poetry of William Blake, and taking care of the summer homes of wealthy Warsaw residents. Her reputation as a crank and a recluse is amplified by her not-so-secret preference for the company of animals over humans. Then a neighbor, Big Foot, turns up dead. Soon other bodies are discovered, in increasingly strange circumstances. As suspicions mount, Janina inserts herself into the investigation, certain that she knows whodunit. If only anyone would pay her mind.Drive your Plow Over the Bones of the Dead Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests! An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: One Village, One Book
events.description: Our book club meets monthly at the library to discuss books set in Oakland and the Bay Area or by Bay Area authors. We select our books from a variety of genres and eras so you’re sure to find something you’re interested in. We’ll get to learn more about our city, meet some neighbors and discuss what we’ve read. This year we’re mostly alternating fiction and non-fiction books. Meetings are the first Tuesday of the month (note the change from 2025!) from 6:30-7:30pm. Come to one discussion, a few or all of them! No pre-registration or commitment is required – all we ask is that you’ve read at least some of the book and are ready to discuss it. Here’s our 2026 schedule: (Quoted descriptions are from the library catalog unless otherwise noted.) January 6, 2026: Sourdough by Robin Sloan (fiction)We’re kicking off the year with a quick read about the very Bay Area intersection of tech and food culture. Lois is an exhausted software engineer who is gifted a very special sourdough starter by her favorite bakers when they must flee the country. Pretty soon she’s part of a mysterious underground food scene at the former Alameda Naval Air Station. February 3, 2026: Family Style: Memories of An American From Vietnam by Thien Pham (graphic memoir)The book that got the most votes from our members on our year-end poll! “Told through the lens of meaningful food and meals, this graphic novel chronicles the author’s childhood immigration to America, where food takes on new meaning as he and his family search for belonging, for happiness and for the American dream.” Pham is a graphic novelist, comic artist and educator based in Oakland. March 3, 2026: Deep Care: The Radical Activists Who Provided Abortions, Defied the Law, and Fought to Keep Clinics Open by Angela Hume (non-fiction)“The story of the radical feminist networks who worked outside the law to defend abortion. Deep Care follows generations of activists and clinicians who orbited the Women’s Choice clinic in Oakland from the early 1970s until 2010, as they worked underground and above ground, in small cells and broad coalitions and across political movements with grit, conviction, and allegiances of great trust to do what they believed needed to be done–despite the law, when required.” April 7, 2026: Moonglow by Michael Chabon (fiction)“A man bears witness to his grandfather’s deathbed confessions, which reveal his family’s long-buried history and his involvement in a mail-order novelty company, World War II and the space program.” Most of it isn’t set in Oakland, but it is the only book on this list that specifically mentions Montclair Library! May 5, 2026: Automatic Noodle by Annalee Newitz (science fiction)Our May read is a slim 163 pages, but packed with interesting ideas. This book is super popular as of December 2025, so you might want to get your hold in early. “In this semicozy tale set in a dystopian near future, robots open their own restaurant, build an unusual found family and achieve personal growth. A few years after the war that led to California’s secession from the United States, four robots awake in the flooded San Francisco takeout place where they were contracted to work.” (Kirkus) “Emboldened by newly granted civil rights from the California government, the bots reboot their lives in classic Bay Area form: by opening a noodle shop…In many ways, Newitz’s near-future San Francisco resembles today’s city, with not-so-subtle nods to AI anxieties, queer identity and the modern-day immigrant experience. It is, in the words of KQED’s Alexis Madrigal, ‘The most San Francisco book that has ever been or could ever have been written.’” (Leah Worthington) (Ebook listed separately) June 2, 2026: Moneyball by Michael Lewis (non-fiction)Baseball season is in full swing, which makes this the perfect time to read Michael Lewis’s nuanced exploration of “how Billy Beene, the general manager of the Oakland Athletics, used a new kind of thinking to build a successful and winning baseball team without spending enormous sums of money.” July 7, 2026: Girls Girls Girls by Shoshana von Blanckensee (fiction)In the summer of 1996, best friends (and secret girlfriends), Hannah and Sam drive from “New York, to the fabled queer paradise of San Francisco, free from the harsh gazes of their neighbors and the stifling demands of Hannah’s devout Orthodox Jewish mother. In San Francisco, they will finally be together as a real couple, out in the open, around other queer people.” But soon they’re working at a strip club, meeting new people and trying to figure out who just they are. August 4, 2026: Power Hungry: Women of the Black Panther Party and Freedom Summer and Their Fight to Feed a Movement by Suzanne Cope (non-fiction)Journey back with us to the summer of 1969 with the story of the free breakfast movement, grocery giveaways and other nurturing mutual aid programs started by the Black Panthers. “NYU writing professor Cope rescues two female activists from obscurity in this intriguing look at the role that food played in the civil rights and Black Power movements.” If you read One Crazy Summer with us in 2025, you’ll enjoy learning the backstory to the Panthers’ food program. September 1, 2026: Kasher in the Rye by Moshe Kasher (memoir)Go back to school with this “hilarious memoir about the absurdity of addiction.” “When he was a young boy, Kasher’s mother took him on a vacation to the West Coast. Well it was more like an abduction…They moved to Oakland, California. That’s where the real fun begins, in the war zone of Oakland Public Schools. Those early years read like part Augusten Burroughs, part David Sedaris, with a touch of Jim Carrol….Brutally honest and laugh-out-loud funny, Kasher…finds humor in even the most horrifying situations.” October 6, 2026: 107 Days by Kamala Harris (memoir)Hopefully by October the miles-long hold list for this book will have settled down enough for you to get a copy! “For the first time, and with surprising and revealing insights, Kamala Harris tells the story of one of the wildest and most consequential presidential campaigns in American history.” (NPR) November 3, 2026: The Giveaway: The Clay Blackburn Story by Owen Hill (crime fiction)We have something noir-ish by an Oakland author for those long November nights. “Hill’s crime fiction is whip-smart, stylish, subversive, hilarious and just dripping with East Bay color, culture and landmarks. Sweet and sour like a perfect Negroni, Hill has invented a new kind of East Bay Noir — the fog rolls in late, the golden afternoon glow somehow both luxurious and ominous in the class war that never sleeps.” (Recommendation from Tally Ho! Books via Oaklandside) December 1, 2026: Sign My Name to Freedom by Betty Reid Soskin (memoir)Reid Soskin, famous for being the oldest park ranger in the history of the National Park Service while working at the Rosie the Riveter National Park in Richmond, has been witness to a wide sweep of American history. She grew up in the Bay Area, raised four kids, ran a record store in Berkeley and more. “Betty just turned 104 and her memoir gives a fascinating look at African Americans migration, the Rosie the Riveter story, and beyond through the Civil Rights era.” (recommender) (Ebook and audiobook listed separately) « See our 2025 books here »« See our 2024 books here »« See our 2023 books here »Learn more about Montclair Library and its Friends group, FOML, here: About | Friends of Montclair Library

events.description: Quiet Book Club offers a tranquil alternative for book lovers who enjoy the idea of a book club, but often scramble to finish the book or don’t want the pressure of conversation. events.title: Getting Cozy Book Club
events.description: If you've never read a cozy mystery before or if you have read thousands, join us each month as we read and discuss the first book in a series. We will meet on the 2nd Thursday of the month at 10 am. What is a cozy mystery? The crime-solver is an amateur sleuth, who eavesdrops, gathers clues, and uses their native intelligence and knowledge of their community to solve the crime. Cozy mysteries are generally considered “gentle” books and most have no graphic violence. The setting is often a small town full of colorful characters.Series frequently have a prominent thematic element introduced by the detective's job, pet, or hobby.These books are plot and character driven with an emphasis on solving puzzles rather than suspense. Our chosen book with serve as our theme for the meeting, we will talk about other book series with a similar theme as well as the recipes, crafts, and more included in many cozies.

events.title: Fairfax Book Club: On the Wings of a Cuckoo by Kokila Byrne
events.description: The Fairfax Library Thursday Morning Book Club will meet in-person every other month to discuss a new book. Everyone is welcome to attend whether or not you have read the selected book for that meeting. This is a drop-in event. We gather in person at the Fairfax Library for this conversation.We will be discussing our selection: On the Wings of a Cuckoo by Kokila Byrne.On the Wings of a Cuckoo is written by Fairfax's own Kokila Byrne. Join our discussion and gain insight from the author herself!"In 1974, Kokila (Mary Ann Byrne) embarked on a life-changing journey, leaving her teaching career in the United States to travel to India with her five-year-old son. Over the next decade, they immersed themselves in a rich tapestry of diverse cultures, from the beaches of Goa in southern India to the majestic Himalayan mountains. After three transformative years, they joined the ashram of Bhagwan Shree Rajneesh, where Kokila welcomed the birth of her daughter. During their time in the East, Kokila and her family explored many countries. This extraordinary adventure shaped her worldview and deepened her connection to the human spirit. Returning to the United States in 1984, Kokila resumed her teaching career, yet her wanderlust and quest for understanding remained undiminished. Each summer, she embarked on new journeys to distant lands, continually weaving her life with rich experiences and a global perspective. Kokila's writing reflects profound insights gained from years of exploration, spiritual growth, and cross-cultural encounters. Her work invites readers to join her on a journey of discovery-both outward into the world and inward to the depths of the soul."--AmazonTo acquire a copy of this book, please call Fairfax Library at 415-453-8151.Fairfax Book Club is sponsored by the Friends of the Fairfax Library. events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Join us to share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. Everyone in My Family Has Killed Someone by Benjamin Stevenson SYNOPSIS: Some of us, the high achievers, have killed more than once. I'm not trying to be dramatic, but it is the truth. Some of us are good, others are bad, and some just unfortunate. I'm Ernest Cunningham. Call me Ern or Ernie. I wish I'd killed whoever decided our family reunion should be at a ski resort, but it's a little more complicated than that. Have I killed someone? Yes. I have. Who was it? Let's get started.

events.title: Graphic Novel Book Club
events.description: Monthly book discussion on a graphic novel selected by the group! This program is for readers age 16 and up. 2025-2026 titles: September 11: Understanding Comics by Scott McCloud (1994), 224 pagesOctober 9: Artificial: A Love Story by Amy Kurzweil (2023), 368 pagesNovember 13: The Road by Cormac McCarthy (2024), 160 pagesDecember 11: The House by Paco Roca (2019), 134 pagesJanuary 8: In: A Graphic Novel by Will McPhail (2021), 272 pagesFebruary 12: Special presentations by book club members who are authors: Mimi & Soni Discover Dussehra Legends by Devika Joglekar (2019), Kye in the Valley of Gold by Kyle Hurlbut (2024), Smiling Through the Mask by Daniele Archambault (2022), as well as artist Deborah J. Miller who will be sharing her current project.March 12: Feeding Ghosts by Tessa Hulls (2024), 400 pagesApril 9: Yellow Cab by Benoit Cohen (2022), 168 pagesMay 14: 40 Men and 12 Rifles: Indochina 1954 by Marcelino Truong (2023), 296 pagesTitles can be borrowed from the Palo Alto City Library and through our Link+ system - click on the links above or search in our catalog for more information. Meetings are held in person in the Midtown Room at Mitchell Park Library and facilitated by Danièle Archambault. The GNBC has been meeting since 2015! Danièle Archambault, Ph.D. (Linguistics), is a researcher, author, and educator who is interested in comics as a way to document a society's cultural and linguistic landscape. Originally from Montreal, Quebec, she now lives in Palo Alto, where she is an artist in residence at the Cubberley Artist Studio Program, a program of the City of Palo Alto. In the past few years, she has published several full-color comic books, graphic novels, and electronic books. Ms. Archambault is a regular guest speaker on documenting culture and dialects through comics at various educational and cultural institutions. She teaches Visual Storytelling at the Palo Alto Art Center, and at other educational venues. http://danielebd.com/

In March, we'll discuss Alice's Adventures in Wonderland by Lewis Carroll, and in April we'll dive into Where the Wild Things Are by Maurice Sendak. Participants will reflect on the messages of each book and share their insights in a supportive environment. Presented in partnership with Lantern Book Club. Teens welcome. برای یک گفت‌وگوی جذاب و معنا‌دار به زبان‌های انگلیسی، فارسی و فرانسوی، به ما بپیوندید.در ماه مارس، کتاب «آلیس در سرزمین عجایب» و در ماه آوریل، «جایی که موجودات وحشی هستند» را بررسی خواهیم کرد.شرکت‌کنندگان درباره پیام هر کتاب فکر می‌کنند و دیدگاه‌های خود را در فضایی دوستانه و حمایتی به اشتراک می‌گذارند.این برنامه با همکاری گروه Lantern Book Club برگزار می‌شود.نوجوانان عزیز، خوش‌آمدید!Pour une discussion enrichissante et captivante en anglais, farsi et français, rejoignez-nous. En mars, nous discuterons de Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland et en avril de Where the Wild Things Are. Les participantes réfléchiront aux messages de chaque livre et partageront leurs points de vue dans un environnement chaleureux et bienveillant. events.title: Teen Advisory Board @ Main TeenZone
events.description: Interested in creating your own teen programs or events? Got a club you've always wanted to start or a skill you'd love to share? Have an eye for design and ideas for book displays or holiday celebrations? Drop in to our monthly Teen Advisory Board (TAB) meetings on the second *Friday* of the month at the Main Library TeenZone to learn more about what's possible at TAB and start collaborating with other teens. Snacks provided!TAB is a youth-led, librarian-supported volunteer group for youth in 7th—12th grades who are excited to take an active role in shaping what we offer to teens at the Main TeenZone. As a TAB member, you get the opportunity to: Make real your ideas for the TeenZone as a place for teens, by teens! Develop real-world skills and experience that you can use in job and college applications Fulfill your community service hour requirements Enjoy food, fun, and friendship at our monthly meet-ups Fill out a volunteer application here. Get accessibility information.

events.description: Looking for a space to heal, grow, and connect? You’re not alone—and this might be the place for you. Join the Knyaw Book Club for early to mid-level Knyaw professionals to reflect on and unpack the cultures we've grown out of—and into. It’s a space for conversation, curiosity, and community. Every experience matters. No story is too small. The first session will be Saturday November 8th, 10AM at Arlington Hills Public Library. The group will establish which Saturdays work best for everyone at the first meeting.

events.title: Bite Sized Book Club- Tread of Angels
events.description: Think you're too busy for a book club? Think again! Schlafly's "Bite-Sized Books" features diverse selections under 250 pages - a perfect fit for busy students, parents and professionals. Whether you're looking to try new genres, sample a different perspective or fit in a quick read during your kiddo's nap time, this club has you covered. Join us for our Saturday one-hour discussions every month. And if our current theme isn't for you, don't fret - check back in July for our next topic!This unit's focus is "Fantastical History", and April's selection is "Tread of Angels" by Rebecca Roanhorse (201 pages). This read has elements of fantasy, historical fiction, and mystery. Description - "Celeste, a card sharp with a need for justice, takes on the role of advocatus diaboli, to defend her sister Mariel, accused of murdering a Virtue, a member of the ruling class of this mining town, in a new world of dark fantasy."

Or perhaps you want to improve your skills by doing fun creative writing exercises! If that sounds like a good time to you, drop by this writing club! Everyone is welcome - from beginners to experienced authors. This group is for teens only. In this workshop we will: -Read targeted pieces to generate ideas about theme, plot twists, and writing techniques. -Peer review each other's work. -Do fun writing exercises. -Encourage each other to conquer writer's block and gain confidence in sharing our writing. This workshop will take place on two Saturdays per month. Registration recommended but not required. Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. events.title: High Intermediate ESL Book Club - The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1
events.description: Our high intermediate ESL Book Club will read The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1 by Martha Wells. This book club is best for high intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Sheila Jurik. Meeting datesJanuary 12, 26 (No class Jan 19)February 2, 23 (No class Feb 9, 16)March 2, 9, 23, 30 (No class Mar 16)April 13, 20, 27 (No class Apr 6)Meeting time: 10:15am - 11:45amWhere: In the library - your book club leader will tell you which tables. Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1 description:On a distant planet, a team of scientists are conducting surface tests, shadowed by their Company-supplied ‘droid ― a self-aware SecUnit that has hacked its own governor module, and refers to itself (though never out loud) as “Murderbot.” Scornful of humans, all it really wants is to be left alone long enough to figure out who it is (and to watch its favorite show in its downtime.) But when a neighboring mission goes dark, it’s up to the scientists and Murderbot to get to the truth.

events.title: Manga Monday Book Club
events.description: Enjoy reading manga? Join other teens to discuss stories and characters in our new Manga Monday Club! We will introduce Volume 1 of a series to get you started on your next reading adventure. We encourage teens to read at least Volume 1, whether that is a physical copy from your own manga collection, from our library, or borrowed from a friend. Digital reading also counts; we will be happy to have you participate in our discussion if you have read an e-copy. Physical copies of Volume 1 will be available for in person pick-up at the Enon Branch on a first come, first served basis. Check out Libby and Hoopla for available digital copies. Scheduled for the second Monday of the month. For teens. This month's selection: Witch Hat Atelier by Kamome Shirahama Summary: In a world where everyone takes wonders like magic spells and dragons for granted, Coco is a girl with a simple dream: She wants to be a witch. But everybody knows magicians are born, not made, and Coco was not born with a gift for magic. Resigned to her un-magical life, Coco is about to give up on her dream to become a witch...until the day she meets Qifrey, a mysterious, traveling magician. After secretly seeing Qifrey perform magic in a way she's never seen before, Coco soon learns what everybody "knows" might not be the truth, and discovers that her magical dream may not be as far away as it may seem...” Rated 13 +

events.title: Cookbook Book Club
events.description: Do you love books and food? Are you looking for a fun, low-cost way to increase your cooking skills, try new recipes and make some new foodie friends? Then perhaps our new "Cookbook Book Club" is for you!Our book club hostess is Chef JoEllen Hothem. She is a self-educated home chef who began exploring food ways and cooking as a child through the "Mud Pies and Other Recipes" by Marjorie Winslow. She loves to learn about world cuisines through reading and travel. A firm believer in the tremendous health benefits of intentional, healthful eating, in community, she combines her love of food with her public speaking and instructional training to help people learn the principles of taste, flavor and foundational cooking skills, so they can confidently prepare tasty and healthful meals for themselves, family and friends.

events.title: Main Book Club
events.description: Join the Main Book Club in the Olympic Room to discuss the novel, Piranesi by Susanna Clarke.About the book: Piranesi’s house is no ordinary building: its rooms are infinite, its corridors endless, its walls are lined with thousands upon thousands of statues, each one different from all the others. Within the labyrinth of halls an ocean is imprisoned; waves thunder up staircases; rooms are flooded in an instant. But Piranesi is not afraid; he understands the tides as he understands the pattern of the labyrinth itself. He lives to explore the house.But as Piranesi explores, evidence emerges of another person, and a terrible truth begins to unravel, revealing a world beyond the one Piranesi has always known.Copies of Piranesi are available at Main while they last for sign out, or from various branch collections.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club
events.description: Are you learning English and want to practice reading and speaking in a group setting? Then the Intermediate ESL Book Club is the place for you! Join other adults learning English as a Second Language in this fun and engaging club. You'll read one book a month and then come discuss it with the rest of the club. Be ready to read your favorite passages to the group! If the books on this list look too easy, try our Advanced ESL Book Club. Here's the reading list: January: Ghost Boys February: The House on Mango Street March: Come on in April: Soul Lanterns May: The Breadwinner June: Paper Wishes July: The Lost Library August: The Birchbark House September: Rosa Parks, My Story October: The Elephant Girl November: A Night Divided December: The One and Only Ivan Any person needing a reasonable accommodation for a disability to access the benefits of the Library’s services, programs, or activities under the Americans with Disabilities Act should contact the Library by telephone at (972) 292-5669, by email at library@friscotexas.gov, or in writing, not less than five (5) business days prior to the program.

events.title: Friends Adventure Club: Star Wars
events.description: Friends Adventure Club is a sensory-friendly interactive program for adults with neurodiversity and/or intellectual or developmental disabilities. CPL is more than books. It’s a place where everyone can learn, play, and create! We welcome adults with disabilities and their caregivers to create, play games, and enjoy music, trivia, bingo, and guest speakers. Each program is tailored to the audience’s unique interests and ability levels. This inclusive and accessible program is intended for patrons with disabilities aged 18+ with their families and/or caregivers. April's theme is Star Wars, where we will play trivia, bingo, create a themed craft and more. Join us in the Community Room for this fun-filled social program on Thursday, April 16.Attendance at Library events constitutes consent to be photographed for use in print and/or electronic publicity for the Canton Public Library.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Chains (The Seeds of America Trilogy)
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read Chains (The Seeds of America Trilogy) by Laurie Halse Anderson. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Roberta Linsky. Meeting dates: February 26; March 5, 12, 19; April 2, 16; May 7. (No class March 26; April 9, 23, 30.)Meeting time: Thursdays from 10:00am - 11:00amWhere: Tables by the wall posters Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Book description:As the Revolutionary War begins, thirteen-year-old Isabel wages her own fight...for freedom. Promised freedom upon the death of their owner, she and her sister, Ruth, in a cruel twist of fate become the property of a malicious New York City couple, the Locktons, who have no sympathy for the American Revolution and even less for Ruth and Isabel. When Isabel meets Curzon, a slave with ties to the Patriots, he encourages her to spy on her owners, who know details of British plans for invasion. She is reluctant at first, but when the unthinkable happens to Ruth, Isabel realizes her loyalty belongs to whoever can provide her with freedom.

events.title: Book Club Discussion - A Guardian and a Thief
events.description: Want to connect with others over the love of reading? Join our book club to discuss Megha Majumdar's A Guardian and a Thief. This title should be on shelves and ready to borrow approximately 5 weeks prior to the discussion date. Check with a staff member if you have questions on how to access a copy.Book Description"In a near-future Kolkata beset by flooding and blight, Ma, her two year old daughter Mishti, and her elderly father Dadu are just days from leaving the collapsing city behind to join Ma's husband in the home he has been building for them in Ann Arbor, Michigan. After procuring long-awaited passports and visas from the consulate, they pack their bags for the flight to America. But in the morning they awaken to discover that Ma's purse, with all the treasured documents within it, has been stolen. A Guardian and a Thief tells two stories: the story of Ma and her family, their struggle to emigrate to America, and their devastation in the wake of the theft that changes their fate to one of implacable tragedy; and Boomba, the thief, whose hunger and desperation to care for his family drive him to commit a crime whose consequences he cannot fathom. With stunning control and command, Megha Majumdar paints a kaleidoscopic portrait of two families whose destinies become inexorably entangled, wresting compassion from each narrative as the complexities of each character's circumstances-their helplessness in the face of poverty and corruption, and the need to stave off encroaching catastrophe--are captured with clarity and piercing empathy. A masterful new work from one of the most exciting voices of her generation"--Provided by publisher.

events.title: Book Club Discussion - The Names
events.description: Want to connect with others over the love of reading? Join our book club to discuss Florence Knapp's The Names. This title should be on shelves and ready to borrow approximately 5 weeks prior to the discussion date. Check with a staff member if you have questions on how to access a copy.Book Description"A dazzling debut that asks: Can a name shape the course of a life? In the wake of an enormous, history-making storm, Cora sets off with her nine-year-old daughter, Maia, to register her son's birth. Her husband Gordon, a local doctor, respected in the community but a terrifying and controlling presence at home, intends for her to follow his family tradition going back generations, and name the child Gordon. But on the journey there, Cora wonders if it's right to impose the burden of this name and its legacy onto her tiny newborn son. She herself has Julian in mind, and Maia offers up her own suggestion: Bear. What follows are three alternate and alternating versions of both Cora's life and her young son's life shaped by her brave, last-minute choice of name. Spanning thirty-five years, the novel draws us in from the first page, as we follow three unforgettable journeys of one young man, but also his mother, grandmother, and sister. In richly layered prose, The Names explores the painful ripple effects of domestic abuse, the messy ties of family, and the possibilities of autonomy and healing."--Provided by publisher.

events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read The Game by Monica Hughes. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Rianne Hastie Adams. Meeting dates: February 27; March 6, 20, 27; April 3, 17. (No class March 13 and April 10.)Meeting time: Fridays 11:30am - 12:30pm Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. The Game description:It’s the year 2154. Lisse and her friends have been deemed unemployable in the eyes of society. Now they must scavenge the disintegrating city for food and shelter, just to make ends meet. But their dismal existence starts to look up when Lisse and her friends are invited to participate in The Game, a virtual reality experience where they are challenged to survive. But over time, the line between reality and fantasy starts to blur. What started as a simple exercise quickly becomes a test of endurance, trust, and their will to live.

events.title: Unplugged Book Club
events.description: (This is a special Unplugged Book Club session where we will be joined by the hosts of the Environmental Book Club at DeGraaf Nature Center) The Unplugged Book Club is a collaborative book discussion series between ODC Network and Herrick District Library. Throughout 2026, we invite you to read books related to disconnecting from unhealthy tech habits and reconnecting with our community and nature. Want to meet some of the authors? Join the ODC Network's Unplugged: Disconnect to Reconnect speaker series to meet Catherine Price (January 14), Katherine Martinko (February 12), Dr. Scott Sampson (March 3), and Lenore Skenazy (March 17).About the Unplugged: Disconnect to Reconnect speaker series - ODC Network invites the West Michigan community to disconnect from overuse of technology and reconnect with nature and each other, while reintroducing the simple idea that outdoor and community connection is central to thriving people, thriving families, and thriving communities. Learn more at outdoordiscovery.org/unplugged Here is our Unplugged Book Club lineup: April 20th, 6:30-8:00p at DeGraaf Nature Center (600 Graafschap Rd) – Braiding Sweetgrass by Robin Wall Kimmerer (Hosted jointly with the Environmental Book Club) ___ May 11th, 6:30 – 7:30p at Herrick Main (classroom) – An Immense World by Ed Yong ___ June 17th, 6:30-7:30p at DeGraaf Nature Center (600 Graafschap Rd) – How to Know a Person by David Brooks ___ July 13th, 6:30-7:30p at Herrick Main (classroom) – A Walk in the Woods by Bill Bryson ___ August 13th, 10:00-11:00a Herrick Main (classroom) – Digital Minimalism by Cal Newport ___ September 17th, 6:30- 7:30p at Herrick North Branch – The Nature Fix by Florence Williams ___ October 7th, 6:00-7:00p at Herrick Main (classroom) – My Side of the Mountain by Jean Craighead George (All ages, with Youth Services) ___ November 10th, 6:00-7:00p at Herrick Main (classroom) – Wild by Cheryl Strayed

events.title: Tea & Robots Book Club
events.description: Have an appetite for science fiction but don't have time to delve into another space opera? Join us every third Monday of the month to discuss the novellas and short story collections of award-winning sci-fi authors. Through this Spring's offerings of stories, readers will explore a range of topics, from androids to first contact, and from post-apocalyptic to Afrofuturism. Registered participants are welcome to pick up their copies of this month’s book at Divoll. For April, we’re reading excerpts from Lake of Souls, St. Louisan author Ann Leckie’s collection of short stories, both previously published and newly released. The eponymous story, “Lake of Souls,” was a Hugo nominee for best novelette. Leckie’s take on the classic first contact plot, it’s about the friendship between a lone human survivor and an extraterrestrial on a search for meaning.For accessibility accommodations, please call 314-241-2288.

events.title: Books on Tap
events.description: We're bringing book discussions to local bars & breweries. Join us to rant and rave about our favorite books or least favorite tropes. Check our staff booklists for recommendations, come with your own, or listen to what others' are reading! No required book or booklist. Ages 21+.

events.title: Manchester Book Group
events.description: Pick up the next book club selection at the Manchester library service desk following the previous month's meeting on the 3rd Monday of the month. **NOTE Meetings in January and February each year are held on the FOURTH Monday of the month due to the Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Day and President's Day holidays This month we are discussing: Dear Edward by Ann Napolitano.From the Publisher:What does it mean not just to survive, but to truly live?One summer morning, twelve-year-old Edward Adler, his beloved older brother, his parents, and 183 other passengers board a flight in Newark headed for Los Angeles. Among them are a Wall Street wunderkind, a young woman coming to terms with an unexpected pregnancy, an injured veteran returning from Afghanistan, a business tycoon, and a free-spirited woman running away from her controlling husband. Halfway across the country, the plane crashes. Edward is the sole survivor.Edward’s story captures the attention of the nation, but he struggles to find a place in a world without his family. He continues to feel that a part of himself has been left in the sky, forever tied to the plane and all of his fellow passengers. But then he makes an unexpected discovery—one that will lead him to the answers of some of life’s most profound questions: When you’ve lost everything, how do you find the strength to put one foot in front of the other? How do you learn to feel safe again? How do you find meaning in your life?

We’ll also feature large-print books and recipes for easy browsing. All abilities are welcome, and activities are optional. Come for what interests you and stay for what feels good.

events.title: Swasey Book Club
events.description: Join us for the Swasey Book Club! April's selection is The Frozen River by Ariel Lawhon. About the book:"A gripping historical mystery based on the real-life diary entries of Martha Ballard, an 18th-century midwife who defied the legal system and wrote herself into American history. Maine, 1789: When the Kennebec River freezes, entombing a man in the ice, Martha Ballard is summoned to examine the body and determine cause of death. As a midwife and healer, she is privy to much of what goes on behind closed doors in Hallowell. Her diary is a record of every birth and death, crime and debacle that unfolds in the close-knit community. When a local physician undermines her conclusion, declaring the death to be an accident, Martha is forced to investigate the shocking murder on her own. Over the course of one winter, as the trial nears, and whispers and prejudices mount, Martha doggedly pursues the truth. Her diary soon lands at the center of the scandal, implicating those she loves, and compelling Martha to decide where her own loyalties lie." Stop into the Swasey branch to secure your book club copy and then join us for the discussion on April 21. We hope you'll join us! The Swasey Book Club selection checks out on a first-come, first-served basis. Supply is limited. Ask library staff for assistance. Call us at (253)280-2970 if you have any questions.
